content: Specimen detail reference image for Eleutherobia variabile (Mushroom soft coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 72, printed page 69. Associated diagnostic features: Colonial soft coral with leathery, swollen, mushroom- shaped head, bearing numerous polyps. The head is clearly distinct from the smooth barren stalk. Sometimes attaching to sponges and shells. Associated colour description: Variable colouring ranging from orange, pale orange, tan, pink, red, yellow or white. Sometimes bicoloured or mottled. Associated size description: Maximum colony height 70 mm (Williams, 1986). Expanded polyps reach 12 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa; 13–470 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Gersemia liltvedi (Stalked cauliflower soft coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 73, printed page 70. Associated diagnostic features: Colonies erect, cauliflower-like in form, arising from one main base from which several stems may arise. Polyps relatively congested at ends of short, narrow terminal branches (observed more readily in wet preserved specimens). Polyps non-retractile with calyces, supporting bundles of polyps, and polyp crowns absent. Associated colour description: Variable. Colonies usually pale beige, white to pink or orange. Associated size description: Colonies reported to range between 56 and 110 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Known from the South Coast of South Africa. This is a temperate genus without zooxanthellae occurring in the 20-2 000 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Anthomastus giganteus (Gigantic soft coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 74, printed page 71. Associated diagnostic features: Large, fleshy, erect polyps arising from one elongate stalk. Polyps of one type (autozoids) being very large and emergent (may not retract) on stalks. Disc-like base often attached to hard substrate or debris. Associated colour description: Pink to orange or red stem with paler (beige, white or pinkish) terminal polyps. May occur as entirely white colony. Associated size description: Maximum size 150 mm. Associated distribution: South and West Coasts. One of the deepest occurring soft corals, recorded to 450 m in South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Melithaea spp. (colourful sea fan) from the species identification page. PDF page 75, printed page 72. Associated diagnostic features: Sea fans with cylindrical or slightly flattened stem and many dichotomous branches. The skeleton is composed of gorgonin (a horn-like protein) and scerites and is stiff but flexible and not brittle. Melithaea spp. branch in one plane, have nodes but no calyces. Polyps monomorphic (1 type), small, retractile and with eight tentacles, seldom visible to the naked eye. Identification of this group is challenging, with the genera Wrightella, Melithea and others requiring microscopic sclerite examination. Associated colour description: Variable and often vivid; commonly white, red, orange, pink or yellow. Associated size description: Usually between 50 and 500 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa, Indo-Pacific; high diversity across a broad depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Thouarella spp. (Bottlebrush soft coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 76, printed page 73. Associated diagnostic features: Bottlebrush-shaped colonies not flattened in one plane. Stiff central rod with many polyp-bearing branches arising from a single main stem. Branching is profuse, pinnate and multi-planar. Polyps can be seen with the naked eye. Large scales present on sides of polyps giving colony a slightly stiff texture, but these are not visible with the naked eye. Frequently has associates including scale worms, brittlestars, fish eggs and larvae. Associated colour description: Most commonly observed in yellow, pale cream or a very pale pink. Associated size description: Variable. Polyps usually 1 to 1.5 mm in length, with colonies reaching 300 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa; at 100-900 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for - - (Bamboo coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 77, printed page 74. Associated diagnostic features: Hollow, calcified, inflexible and segmented axes composed of nodes of horn and solid internodes of non-spicular calcium carbonate; giving ‘bamboo-like’ appearance. Tree-like with fine fragile branches. Specimens brittle, handle with care. Three genera reported from South Africa, Keratoisis species branch from the calcareous nodes and Acanella branches from horny internodes. Chathamisis is the third genus reported from South Africa. Skeleton surface is smooth (not porous or sandpapery as in stylasterine or noble corals). Global taxonomic work underway on this group and further work needed in South Africa. Associated colour description: Polyps are highly variable in colour. Rust-coloured, orange, pink and white colonies noted; when flesh is scraped away the skeleton is revealed with white internodes with brown joints (nodes). Associated size description: Colonies usually ranging between 50 and 300 mm in height; larger in-situ. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan, reported from West and South Coasts of South Africa; 200-4 850 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

embedding_text: - - Bamboo coral Bamboo - Isididae Alcyonacea Anthozoa Subclass: Octocorallia Cnidaria Cnidaria Anthozoa Subclass: Octocorallia Alcyonacea Isididae - specimen reference image photo figure distribution map identification guide Distribution map for - - (Bamboo coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 77, printed page 74. Associated diagnostic features: Hollow, calcified, inflexible and segmented axes composed of nodes of horn and solid internodes of non-spicular calcium carbonate; giving ‘bamboo-like’ appearance. Tree-like with fine fragile branches. Specimens brittle, handle with care. Three genera reported from South Africa, Keratoisis species branch from the calcareous nodes and Acanella branches from horny internodes. Chathamisis is the third genus reported from South Africa. Skeleton surface is smooth (not porous or sandpapery as in stylasterine or noble corals). Global taxonomic work underway on this group and further work needed in South Africa. Associated colour description: Polyps are highly variable in colour. Rust-coloured, orange, pink and white colonies noted; when flesh is scraped away the skeleton is revealed with white internodes with brown joints (nodes). Associated size description: Colonies usually ranging between 50 and 300 mm in height; larger in-situ. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan, reported from West and South Coasts of South Africa; 200-4 850 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Anthoptilum grandiflorum (Large sea pen) from the species identification page. PDF page 78, printed page 75. Associated diagnostic features: Large, whip-like central stem (calcareous rod/rachis), sometimes protruding from the top of specimens. Tentacled polyps in short, oblique rows, united at base, forming five to ten polyps per row. Polyps fused into small ‘leaves’, arranged in two opposing lateral rows on central stem. Base of stem (peduncle) inflated to assist rooting in soft sediment. Peduncle stout and robust, not more than 1/5th total colony length. Associated colour description: Variable; orange to pink or brown, but also bright red. Associated size description: Variable; colonies mostly up to 600 m in height, but can reach in excess of 1 m. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan, West Coast of South Africa; at 200-2 500 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Umbellula lindahli (Umbrella sea pen) from the species identification page. PDF page 79, printed page 76. Associated diagnostic features: Polyps arranged in cluster at end of long, thin stalk (rachis), giving umbrella-like appearance. Thin (1-2 mm width) rachis conspicuously quadrangular in transverse section. Terminally clustered, slender polyps have eight to ten autozooids, each 20-30 mm in length. Sclerites (requiring microscopic examination) are absent. Associated colour description: Pale pink to orange in colour. Associated size description: 250 to 300 mm in length. Reportedly can reach up to more than 1 m in length. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan (490-2 963 m). Recorded on West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Halipteris africana (Whip sea pen) from the species identification page. PDF page 80, printed page 77. Associated diagnostic features: Whip-like colony. Peduncle (lower section without polyps including the end bulb) stout with stiff internal axis that is rounded to rounded-quadrangular. Polyps arranged in numerous oblique rows (up to three to seven per row, usually four to six). Associated colour description: Pale orange, yellow to white rachis with deep purple to red-brown polyps. Associated size description: Approximately 10-15 mm wide. Length 200- 1 550 mm. Peduncle length usually about 200 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Reported from the Atlantic Coast of Africa between 400-700 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Actinoptilum molle (radial sea pen) from the species identification page. PDF page 81, printed page 78. Associated diagnostic features: Cylindrical, plump and sausage-shaped colony. Densely clustered polyps project along approximately three quarters of body. Radial symmetry of the rachis, which tapers gradually to a rounded apex. Polyps distributed evenly on all sides, often forming longitudinal rows. Thick peduncle, tapering gradually, usually 1/5th to 1/3rd total colony length. Associated colour description: Highly variable; white, yellow, red, orange, pink to purple and brown. The peduncle varies between yellow, white, pinkish or brownish. Associated size description: Up to 240 mm, but most in the range from 60 to 80 mm in length. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. Cape Columbine to Inhaca Island (Mozambique). Known depth range 12-333 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Cavernularia spp. (Small sea pen) from the species identification page. PDF page 82, printed page 79. Associated diagnostic features: Colony is club-shaped with radial symmetry, may be cylindrical and club-shaped (clavate) or capitate (forming a head). Densely clustered polyps on approximately half of body evenly distributed on all sides. Thick peduncle tapering gradually to rounded apex. Peduncle slightly swollen near the junction with the rachis. Associated colour description: Pale orange, cream to white or grey. Associated size description: 20-70 mm in length. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cerianthid spp. (Burrowing anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 83, printed page 80. Associated diagnostic features: Cerianthids have a crown of two whorls of different sized tentacles. The outer whorl consists of large, long tentacles that are used for food capture and defence. The smaller, shorter, inner tentacles are held more erect. Cerianthids are also called ‘tube- dwelling anemones’ because they live in long tubes buried in soft sediment, with only their tentacles exposed on the seabed surface. They readily withdraw their tentacles deep inside the tube on the slightest level of disturbance and are therefore not often captured in a trawl net. Associated colour description: Variable. Associated size description: Up to 30 cm in diameter when tentacles are expanded. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated size description: Up to 30 cm in diameter when tentacles are expanded. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Bolocera kerguelensis (Blush/coral anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 84, printed page 81. Associated diagnostic features: Soft, smooth body wall that does not retain shape well out of water. Usually covered in slime. Up to 160 long tentacles, usually somewhat retracted on deck but still visible. Tentacles are often shed (released from the oral disc when disturbed) and this is diagnostic (also known as the tentacle-shedding anemone). Dark pink in colour, with smooth column which becomes horizontally wrinkled in the preserved state. Associated colour description: Variable but usually dark pink, orange to brown. Colour uniform with tentacles and body colour similar. Associated size description: Up to 100 mm height but small individuals are common. Preserved diameter of column 30-35 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa; 81-750 m. Common. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Actinauge granulata (White anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 85, printed page 82. Associated diagnostic features: Tough, leathery body wall, cylindrical in shape with warty projections or ridges, often covered with fine sediment. Usually with 96 tentacles, mostly or completely retracted when on deck, but will emerge when placed in seawater and relaxed. Associated colour description: White exterior body walls, often covered with fine sediment. Maroon or red/brown mouth. Associated size description: Large, 100 mm height. Diameter of column up to 60 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Actinoscyphia plebeia (Maroon mouth anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 86, printed page 83. Associated diagnostic features: Fairly toughened body wall, smooth and slimy. Tentacles always well retracted on deck but occur on two distinct lobes. Between 96 and 140 short pointed tentacles arranged in two or three cycles close to the margin. Associated colour description: Pale pink to white, or cloudy grey on outside body wall, with mottled maroon/brown colouration patterns. Deep maroon colour tentacles visible inside of two lobes. Associated size description: Up to 100 mm height. Pedal disc 25-85 mm. Associated distribution: Mainly West Coast of South Africa (recorded once on South Coast). Recently reported for the first time in South Africa based on Department of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (DAFF) collections. Known depth 128-866 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Actinostola capensis (Pink/Orange jelly anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 87, printed page 84. Associated diagnostic features: Large anemone with fairly toughened body wall, pink to orange in colour with many (up to 450) short tentacles. When contracted, tentacles not completely covered by column. Cup-shaped with the base narrower than mouth, which may form lobes. Secretes watery slime. Distinct sucker-type foot/disc. Acontia (threadlike defence organs) absent in this genus. Associated colour description: Pink to pale orange, often described as flesh or rose coloured. Tentacles darker than body wall. Associated size description: Up to 150 mm height. Oral disc 40-155 mm. Pedal disc 35-75 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, abundant species. 81-1 005 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Isophellia algoaensis (rugby ball anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 89, printed page 86. Associated diagnostic features: Small, solid, oval-shaped anemone. Often has visible pale bands running longitudinally along length of body wall. Up to 96 short tentacles with inner tentacles longer than outer. Acontia (threadlike defence organs) present. Can have sediment particles sticking to base where buried in sand and may invaginate at base when removed from substrate (giving tapered rugby-ball shape at both ends). Associated colour description: Pale pink to orange with white/lighter bands visible. Sometimes translucent. Tentacles orange. Associated size description: Up to 40 mm in height. Associated distribution: West Coast, Hondeklip Bay to South Coast, East London; depth range of 14 -1 240 m reported. More common on West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Up to 96 short tentacles with inner tentacles longer than outer. Acontia (threadlike defence organs) present. Can have sediment particles sticking to base where buried in sand and may invaginate at base when removed from substrate (giving tapered rugby-ball shape at both ends). Associated colour description: Pale pink to orange with white/lighter bands visible. Sometimes translucent. Tentacles orange. Associated size description: Up to 40 mm in height. Associated distribution: West Coast, Hondeklip Bay to South Coast, East London; depth range of 14 -1 240 m reported. More common on West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Amphianthus capensis (rock/Volcano/Splitting anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 90, printed page 87. Associated diagnostic features: Short, squat, pale anemone with up to 110 small, thin tentacles that are bright red/orange/pink. Often attach to stones or other hard objects. Wide adherent pedal disk also allows this species to attach to octocorals. Acontia (white defensive threads) present that may be triggered when disturbed. Note bumps (mesogleal papillae) along oral margin. Associated colour description: Pale orange/pink with bright red/orange tentacles. Colour diagnostic. Associated size description: Up to 30 mm width by 30 mm height. Pedal disc diameter 25 mm. Associated distribution: West Coast, Port Nolloth to South Coast, Port Elizabeth; reported from 12-623 m depth. One record from Sodwana (12 m), South Africa, may be misidentified. Also reported from Alaska. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Halcurias capensis (ridged anemone) from the species identification page. PDF page 91, printed page 88. Associated diagnostic features: Body pale and firm, 30-68 bright orange and fairly short tentacles, rarely withdrawn into the body. Column stout and smooth, with distinguishing longitudinal ridges running the length of the column (not always evident in live specimens). Lacks acontia. Associated colour description: Pale body, often yellow, bright orange to red tentacles and oral disc. Associated size description: Height 10-25 mm. Preserved pedal disk 3-22 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Known from depths of 25-329 m. Endemic. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Lophelia pertusa (reef-building cold water coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 92, printed page 89. Associated diagnostic features: Solid calcified branching skeleton, forming three-dimensional colonies or matrices. Skeleton calcareous, hard and brittle, giving glassy appearance. Each branch bearing terminal coral polyp with a single (unequal monostomaeous) budding giving an “r” shape rather than a “v” shape. Associated colour description: Variable; yellow, orange to pink or white when live, dead colonies being white, pinkish or brownish. Associated size description: Variable; colony height of 10 m reported. Associated distribution: Semi-cosmopolitan, at 39-2 775 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Solenosmilia cf. variabilis (Thicket coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 93, printed page 90. Associated diagnostic features: Large bushy colonies, equal three-dimensional branching, with dichotomous (dividing in two) branching of terminal polyp cups in a ‘V’ shape or approximately equal-sized branches. Intra- tentacular branching (i.e. new polyps added to the oral disc within the ring of tentacles). Texture of corallum smooth or costate (ridged). Septa (longitudinal partitions or plates within corallite) arranged normally (i.e. never bend and fuse into a Pourtalès plan). Associated colour description: Pink to beige (live), brownish white when dead. Associated size description: Reef-building species. Can form dense thickets standing tens of metres off seabed. More than a ton has been trawled on occasions. Associated distribution: Semi-cosmopolitan, South Coast of South Africa; at 220-2 165 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Goniocorella dumosa (Fine bridge coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 94, printed page 91. Associated diagnostic features: Small, highly branched, bushy colonies, with adjacent branches often linked with hollow tubular bridges (circled in red). Branching is extratentacular (i.e. new polyps are added to the oral disc outside the ring of tentacles). Polyps tend to branch at right angles and branching is apart from any calice (i.e. the branches divide further away from calices than in other thicket-forming taxa). Associated colour description: Brownish; white in museum collections. Associated size description: May form very dense large thickets. Associated distribution: In South Africa reported from between 86 and 760 m on the South Coast and from KwaZulu-Natal. Also known from New Zealand, Indonesia and Korea (88-1 488 m). Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Caryophyllia / Trochocyathus spp. (Small solitary tusk coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 95, printed page 92. Associated diagnostic features: Small cup, with twisted, pointed (ceratoid) base, ending with flat, cylindrical top. Concentric radially arranged septa in oral cavity and central portion (columella) composed of a series of twisted lamellae (fasicular) in Carophyllia. Always solitary with indication of a firm attachment point. Corallum often curved. Caryophyllia (Caryophyllia) (left) has a set of twisted plates in the centre (i.e. fascicular), whereas Trochocyathus has a papillose centre (i.e. series of rods). Associated colour description: White or beige, with tint of orange or pink at base. Associated size description: From 10 to 40 mm wide, up to 50 mm high. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan; West and South Coasts of South Africa. More common in deep water (> 300 m). Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

Associated colour description: White or beige, with tint of orange or pink at base. Associated size description: From 10 to 40 mm wide, up to 50 mm high. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan; West and South Coasts of South Africa. More common in deep water (> 300 m). Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Desmophyllum, Caryophyllia, Balanophyllia, Rhizosmilia, Rhizopsammia and others (cup coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 96, printed page 93. Associated diagnostic features: Cup corals of variable size and shape (usually between 15 mm and 150 mm length) from cylindrical, oval to serpentine. These corals may occur in clumps and it may be challenging to determine whether solitary or colonial and to genus level on deck. Desmophyllum are large solitary cup corals with a calice that is elliptical in shape, septa that are never fused and no columella. These corals may fuse at the base giving the impression of colonial corals. Rhizosmilia are colonial corals that branch from a stolon (often with massive pedicel) and they have a columella. Rhizopsammia colonies are connected by stolons but may appear solitary. Like Balanophyllia, they have some fusing of septa (Pourtalès plan). Javania spp. have a very smooth texture of the coral wall (theca). Associated colour description: White. Associated size description: Up to 200 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa, extending into very deep water. Semi-cosmopolitan. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cladopsammia and Eguchipsammia cf. spp. (right angled corals) from the species identification page. PDF page 97, printed page 94. Associated diagnostic features: Small bushy colonies, formed by extra-tentacular budding (branching away from any calyx and at close to right angles) from a common short base. Polyps fleshy with slimy tissue. The genera Cladopsammia and Eguchipsammia have colonies with septa arranged in a Pourtalès plan (septa bend and fuse). They are difficult to distinguish on deck but Eguchipsammia has a longer base and does not attach firmly to substrate. Current taxonomic work on this family is underway in South Africa. The more distinct ridging on the corallum and the branching at right angles may or may not be distinguishing features of Cladopsammia. Associated colour description: Orange or yellow, but may occur in other colours. Associated size description: Small colonies of 50-100 mm in South Africa. These taxa are not reef-forming but can comprise coral gardens (i.e dense cover). Associated distribution: Only known from the South Coast of South Africa, Indo-Pacific and Atlantic; at 0-470 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Enallopsammia rostrata (Zigzag coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 98, printed page 95. Associated diagnostic features: Colonial, arborescent (tree-like growth) coral with extra-tentacular branching which occurs below the calice. Large calices on one side of the colony and normally arranged septa (i.e. do not bend and fuse to form Pourtalés plan). Texture of septa and theca (skeletal walls of corallites) rough. Associated colour description: Observed live in yellow or white. Associated size description: Total colony height of more than 400 mm observed in situ. Associated distribution: South Coast of South Africa, deeper than 110 m. Globally 110-2 165 m. Also found in New Zealand. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Unidentified spp. (Deep daisy coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 99, printed page 96. Associated diagnostic features: Colonial coral with corallites arising from a common base. This species superficially resembles Coenocyathus (Family Carophyllidae), other genera in the Family Rhizangidae (but axial edges of some septa should be finely dentate ) or even Tubastrea (Dendrophyllidae), but further work is underway to identify this coral. Associated colour description: Skeleton white, pinkish or brownish. Polyps red, yellow, orange. Colour of polyps not distinguishing feature. Associated size description: Colonies. Associated distribution: South Coast of South Africa. Deeper than 110 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Flabellum (Ulocyathus) messum (Folded cup coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 100, printed page 97. Associated diagnostic features: Solitary, hard, laterally compressed (folded in half) cup giving purse-like appearance. Septa alternate between large and small in the calice (cup), giving jagged edges. Growth ridges evident along external wall. Has no obvious pedicel (stem) or base to attach to any substrate. Columella (central column that can be a plate, set of rods or folded membranes) rudimentary or absent. Associated colour description: Light calcareous skeleton with reddish brown to maroon corallum colour distinguishing F. messum from F. lowekeyesi. Associated size description: Variable; but individual corals up to 50 mm. Associated distribution: Reported from West Coast of South Africa. Recorded from 385 to more than 1 000 m elsewhere. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Columella (central column that can be a plate, set of rods or folded membranes) rudimentary or absent. Associated colour description: Light calcareous skeleton with reddish brown to maroon corallum colour distinguishing F. messum from F. lowekeyesi. Associated size description: Variable; but individual corals up to 50 mm. Associated distribution: Reported from West Coast of South Africa. Recorded from 385 to more than 1 000 m elsewhere. content: Specimen detail reference image for Stylaster nobilis (Noble coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 101, printed page 98. Associated diagnostic features: Hard, calcium carbonate skeleton with thick, robust main stem and sparse, thinner secondary dichotomous branches. Main and secondary stems branch in any direction, forming a multidimensional complex. Branch tips blunt and pale. Many tiny, star-shaped pores (these house tiny polyps) are often visible on the main stem. Stylaster nobilis is considerably more robust with thicker branches than other stylasterine hydrocorals. Associated colour description: Usually light pink to rose, or bright pink with characteristic white tips. Associated size description: Colonies can be up to 500 mm in size, but trawled specimens likely to be in pieces of varying size. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Reported from St Helena Bay to the Eastern Cape from 3-174 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Stylaster spp. (Fine branching hydrocoral) from the species identification page. PDF page 102, printed page 99. Associated diagnostic features: Smaller, fine-grained, uniplanar colonies with sparser branching compared with Errina and Errinopsis spp., but more branching than S. nobilis. One (not multiple) attachment to the substrate (may not be visible in trawled specimens) and without anastomosis (branches re-joining to create a lattice). Stylaster subviolacea and S. griseus have blunt tips. S. subviolacea has more prominent and raised cyclosystems (pores) and a coarser texture than S. nobilis. S. bithalamus is white and the branch tips are less blunt as branches continue to divide more finely (sympodial). S. amphiheloides is more delicate with finer tips, although even more delicate species occur. Associated colour description: These species range from white to grey brown and pink. Stylaster subviolacea is light violet or purple with pale tips. S. griseus is light grey to light brown when live and chalky white when dead. S. bithalamus is also brown. S. amphiheloides is uniformly white. Associated size description: This group of species is of moderate to small size. S. griseus is of moderate size, with the largest colony reported to be 70 mm x 60 mm. Associated distribution: S. subviolacea is known from 22-88 m on the West and South Coasts; S. griseus 80-155 m on the South Coast and S. bithalamus from the West and South Coasts (11-155 m). S. amphiheloides is known from 155-1 000 m, with most specimens from deeper than 500 m. All endemic to South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Errina cf. spp. (red hydrocoral) from the species identification page. PDF page 103, printed page 100. Associated diagnostic features: Hard, calcium carbonate skeleton with thick, robust main stem supporting many thinner secondary branches that do not join. May have multiple attachments to substrate. Branching occurs in one plane only and branches do not fuse. Many tiny pores that house polyps may be visible on the main stem. No commensal polychaetes reported for E. capensis although barnacles commonly attached. Associated colour description: Photographed specimen deep pink to red. E. capensis is described as orange with white tips. Associated size description: Colonies collected of 300 mm, but trawled specimens are likely to be in smaller pieces. Associated distribution: The species depicted here was trawled from 103 m on the South Coast of South Africa. Errina spp. are globally distributed from 10 m to up to 1 800 m. E. capensis is known from the South Coast, 40-174 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Errinopsis cf. spp. (Fenestrate hydrocoral) from the species identification page. PDF page 104, printed page 101. Associated diagnostic features: Fine, brittle calcium carbonate colonies that are uniplanar to bushy. Branching fenestrate (highly anastomatic, i.e. branches join into a fine, highly connected lattice or mesh) with multiple attachments to substrate. Rough texture with spiny coenosteum (surface) on close inspection. Microscopic examination needed to confirm identification of hydrocorals. Associated colour description: White or cream. Associated size description: Colony fragments of about 200 x 100 mm and larger specimens observed in-situ (> 330 m). Associated distribution: A rarely reported genus with two known species occurring in South Africa and sub-Antarctic America. In South Africa, E. fenestrata known only from near East London (174-250 m). E. reticulatum not yet reported in South Africa, although this may be the taxa illustrated above. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated distribution: A rarely reported genus with two known species occurring in South Africa and sub-Antarctic America. In South Africa, E. fenestrata known only from near East London (174-250 m). E. reticulatum not yet reported in South Africa, although this may be the taxa illustrated above. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Inferiolabiata cf. spp. (Spiny lace coral) from the species identification page. PDF page 105, printed page 102. Associated diagnostic features: Hard, robust calcium carbonate skeleton with thick, robust main stem and slightly thinner secondary branches. May have polychaete associations. Colonies usually white, although dark brown colony has been collected. Very rough, spiny texture distinguishes this species from the other stylasterine corals commonly collected in South Africa. Associated colour description: White, grey or chocolate brown. Associated size description: Reported size of 50 mm, but a broken colony of more than 200 mm was collected and larger specimens observed in-situ. Associated distribution: South Coast. I. lowei and I. spinosa both reported from depths of less than 155 m. Both known from elsewhere in southern hemisphere. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for - - (hydroid) from the species identification page. PDF page 106, printed page 103. Associated diagnostic features: Fine branching “tree-like” bushy structure; individual polyps not clearly visible (unlike gorgonian polyps), appearing as fine ‘hairs’, fern-like or feathery. The base is often fused to form a “root-like” structure. Difficult to identify to genus or species level without detailed microscope examination. Some species produce larger polyps that appear similar to small anemones. Hydroids are usually more flexible than gorgonians. Associated colour description: Variable; usually brown to white or pale yellow. Associated size description: Highly variable. Associated distribution: Widely distributed within South Africa’s Exclusive Economic Zone. Extracted image dimensions: 192x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Aequorea spp. (Mag jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 107, printed page 104. Associated diagnostic features: The bell is saucer-shaped, transparent and centrally thickened; frequently damaged on capture with margin broken off, leaving only the central “magnifying lens”. When collected whole, a network of uniformly distributed radial canals extend outwards from edge of “lens” to margin. Radial canals are uniform and do not start on the centre portion of disc. Possesses numerous fine marginal tentacles. Associated size description: Up to 200 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: Worldwide, particularly common in the Benguela region, West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Drymonema spp. (Pink meany jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 109, printed page 106. Associated diagnostic features: Relatively thick, flattened dome-shaped bell with patterned branching canals visible (often purple or pink), but not originating from the centre of the bell. Tentacles arise from a broad annular (ring- like) band toward the centre of the subumbrella. Pendulous gonads hang below the subumbrella in complexly folded eversions (turned outwards) of the subumbrellar wall, and the stomach forms over 100 radiate pouches at the bell margin. Rhopalia (small sensory structures) occur in deep subumbrellar niches about a third of the bell radius from the margin toward the mouth. Associated colour description: Base colour opaque white to transparent with pink, purple or brown branching canals. Associated size description: Up to 1 000 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: North and South Atlantic Oceans, Mediterranean Sea. Uncommon along the West Coast of South Africa, but does occur. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Chrysaora fulgida (Benguela compass jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 110, printed page 107. Associated diagnostic features: Compass jelly; rose pink to orange brown in base colour, with 16 darker radiating triangles on upper surface; bell thick. Four long oral arms; spiralled basally, orange/brown in colour. The bell margin is scalloped into 32 lightly pigmented lappets. Possesses 24 delicate, maroon-coloured marginal tentacles (eight persistent). Juveniles are rose-pink in colour, without prominent marks but with eight thin, maroon marginal tentacles. Associated size description: Can be up to 800 mm in diameter, weighing 20 kg, but usually smaller than this. Associated distribution: Regional endemic: common off Namibia (especially so) and the West Coast of South Africa to the Agulhas Bank, South Coast. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Possesses 24 delicate, maroon-coloured marginal tentacles (eight persistent). Juveniles are rose-pink in colour, without prominent marks but with eight thin, maroon marginal tentacles. Associated size description: Can be up to 800 mm in diameter, weighing 20 kg, but usually smaller than this. content: Specimen detail reference image for Chrysaora africana (West African compass jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 111, printed page 108. Associated diagnostic features: Compass jelly; transparent/white in base colour, with 16 darker purple radiating triangles on upper surface: pattern variable. The bell margin is scalloped into 48 (generally purple) lappets. Four long oral arms, white in colour. Individuals possess 40 persistent, ribbon-like marginal tentacles that are purple in colour. Juveniles have similar colour markings to adults. Associated size description: Up to 400 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Uncommon off South Africa but more common off Namibia: range extends up the West Coast of Africa to the Gulf of Guinea. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Chrysaora agulhensis (Agulhas Bank compass jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 112, printed page 109. Associated diagnostic features: Compass jelly: transparent/white in base colour with 16 faintly darker brown/purple radiating triangles on the upper surface; variable in pattern; centre of bell clear; with numerous white spots. Four long, semi-spiralled oral arms, uniformly white in colour in smaller specimens, but base may be red/brown in larger individuals. The bell margin is scalloped into 32 strongly pigmented purple/brown lappets. Animals possess 24 persistent, robust, ribbon-like marginal tentacles (expanded at base) that are white in colour. Juveniles resemble adults in colouration. Associated size description: Up to 400 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Endemic, commonly occurring from Table Bay (West Coast) to Port Elizabeth (South Coast). Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pelagia noctiluca (Pink stripe/stinger jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 113, printed page 110. Associated diagnostic features: The bell is translucent, tinged slightly pink, and covered with fine warts. The bell margin has four short, translucent oral arms. Animals possess eight long, persistent pink tentacles. Gonads form four crescents in bell centre; clearly visible and pink in colour. Painful sting; exercise caution. Associated size description: Up to 150 mm in bell diameter. Associated distribution: Worldwide; common off the West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cephea sp. (Blue crown jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 114, printed page 111. Associated diagnostic features: Bell thick, blue/purple in colour, with noticeable knobs or warts at centre resembling a crown. No marginal tentacles. Oral arms with long, thin filaments at terminal end. This species not yet encountered in trawl surveys but is likely to be. Associated size description: Up to 500 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Uncommonly reported along the East and South East Coasts of South Africa, between Sodwana Bay and Mossel Bay, Indo-Pacific region. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Eupilema inexpectata (root mouthed jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 115, printed page 112. Associated diagnostic features: Thick dome-shaped bell; opaque and white in colour, often with a slightly blue tinge. The upper surface of bell has a granular texture. Animals lack marginal tentacles. Animals have eight relatively stiff, short (less than bell diameter in length) white oral arms that are fused for more than half their length. The oral arms lack “frills” and appendages terminally and have an epaulette basally. Associated size description: Up to 400 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: Endemic to the Southwestern Cape; predominantly nearshore; uncommon. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Rhizostoma spp. (Barrel jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 116, printed page 113. Associated diagnostic features: Thick dome-shaped bell; opaque and white in colour, often with a slightly blue tinge. The upper surface of bell has a granular texture. Margin of bell scalloped, with between 64 and 80 marginal lappets. Lack marginal tentacles, but have eight oral arms that are fused basally for less than half their length. Oral arms are not stiff and possess “frills” (indicated above) and a club-shaped appendage terminally (indicated above), which may be lost on capture; “frilly” epaulettes present basally. Associated size description: Up to 900 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: Widespread in cool temperate waters of the Atlantic Ocean. Particularly common along the South Coast, but can be found anywhere around South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 238x192.

embedding_text: Rhizostoma spp. Barrel jellyfish Rhizo Rhizostoma Rhizostomatidae Rhizostomeae Scyphozoa Cnidaria Cnidaria Scyphozoa Rhizostomeae Rhizostomatidae Rhizostoma specimen reference image photo figure distribution map identification guide Distribution map for Rhizostoma spp. (Barrel jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 116, printed page 113. Associated diagnostic features: Thick dome-shaped bell; opaque and white in colour, often with a slightly blue tinge. The upper surface of bell has a granular texture. Margin of bell scalloped, with between 64 and 80 marginal lappets. Lack marginal tentacles, but have eight oral arms that are fused basally for less than half their length. Oral arms are not stiff and possess “frills” (indicated above) and a club-shaped appendage terminally (indicated above), which may be lost on capture; “frilly” epaulettes present basally. Associated size description: Up to 900 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: Widespread in cool temperate waters of the Atlantic Ocean. Particularly common along the South Coast, but can be found anywhere around South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Thysanostoma spp. (Purple branching canal jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 117, printed page 114. Associated diagnostic features: Relatively thick, dome-shaped bell; of variable colour but with pattern of branching canals visible. Upper surface of bell has a finely granular texture. Margin of bell scalloped, with up to 64 marginal lappets. Lack marginal tentacles, but have eight long, thin oral arms that are not fused basally. The oral arms lack conspicuous clubs or filaments along their length, but may have a small appendage terminally. Associated size description: Up to 250 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: An Indo-Pacific genus found in subtropical and warm temperate waters. Uncommon along the coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Periphylla periphylla (Purple helmet jellyfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 118, printed page 115. Associated diagnostic features: Bell conical or dome-shaped, with a coronal groove situated around midline; mesoglea (jelly substance) thick, transparent. Stomach and sinuses deep red/ purple in colour. Sixteen lappets at bell margin and 12 rigid tentacles, arranged as four groups of three. Four marginal sense organs. Bioluminescent. Associated size description: Up to 350 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: Circumglobal. Generally deep-water species; uncommon. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Peanut worm (Peanut worm) from the species identification page. PDF page 122, printed page 119. Associated diagnostic features: Sipunculid worms (Peanut worms) are unsegmented marine worms that show bilateral symmetry. Mouth located at anterior end of tubular ‘introvert’ (retractable proboscis). Between 18-24 ciliated tentacles surround mouth for feeding (seldom everted on capture). Introvert is usually retracted into body wall, giving them a peanut shape. Generally firm body texture, often covered with sediment particles. All such species are to be recorded as Peanut worms, FishBoard code ‘Sipunc’. Associated colour description: Variable, often covered with sediment. Associated size description: Variable, but generally not greater than 100 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Global distribution. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Chloeia inermis (Common name Bristle worm) from the species identification page. PDF page 127, printed page 124. Associated diagnostic features: Body fairly fleshy and firm, dorso-ventrally flattened and broadly oval. Long, pale yellow chaetae (bristles) along outer ventral edge, with shorter chaetae along mid-latero dorsal surface. Smooth segmented ventral side (± 30 segments). Mouth parts may extrude in a bulbous type ‘head’. Branched gill pairs (branchiae) visible from segment four in mid-dorsal region (red in colour). Bristles can break off into hands/fingers and be slightly irritating, but not poisonous or dangerous. Large catches of this species sometimes occur. Associated colour description: Pale pink to dark brown with yellow bristles. Protruding mouth parts red in colour. Associated size description: Up to 60 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Hyalinoecia tubicola (Quill worm) from the species identification page. PDF page 128, printed page 125. Associated diagnostic features: Quill worms live inside inflexible straw-like tubes, frequently caught in research trawls. Long, thin body shape with numerous rectangular segments. Three long antennae visible on head. Parapodia (feet) clearly visible, with fine chaetae (bristles) projecting. NOTE: Even if only empty tubes are present, this species must still be recorded with a note explaining that only empty tubes were present under FishBoard code ‘PolTub’. Associated colour description: Pale pink to brown, with iridescent sheen. Associated size description: Can be up to 120 mm in length, but segments often break apart. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa as far as Cape Agulhas in south. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Aphrodita alta (Sea mouse) from the species identification page. PDF page 129, printed page 126. Associated diagnostic features: Large polychaete species with firm, solid, fleshy texture. Body oval, arched dorsally, tapering posteriorly, with 35-45 segments bearing 15 pairs of scales (elytra). Usually curls into a circular or semi- circular shape. Dorsal surface covered with many plates and fine hairs. Strong, stout bristles projecting along margin of dorsal and ventral surfaces. Dorsal surface brown and often coated in fine mud. Ventral surface pale pinkish-white colour. Associated colour description: Dorsal surface brown (muddy), ventral surface pale pink. Associated size description: Up to 60 mm in length. Associated distribution: Mostly West Coast, but can occur along South Coast. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Laetmonice benthaliana (Naked scale worm) from the species identification page. PDF page 130, printed page 127. Associated diagnostic features: Oval-bodied polychaete with very thin, transparent scales covering the dorsal surface. Stout bristle encased in each parapodia (foot), with long filamentous yellow chaetae (bristles) projecting along dorso-lateral edge. Ventral surface clearly segmented, pale yellow in colour. Body fleshy, flexible and soft. Associated colour description: Pale pink, brown to yellow in colour. Associated size description: 40–60 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, mostly in deeper waters along shelf edge. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, mostly in deeper waters along shelf edge. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Macellicephala mirabilis (Purple scale worm) from the species identification page. PDF page 132, printed page 129. Associated diagnostic features: Body short (18 segments). Extending from the head is a very long middle antenna ending in a bulb. Although this is a scale worm, the scales are deciduous, therefore not always present. First few parapodia projecting forward. Associated colour description: Body purple, with lighter edges to the parapodia and antenna. Associated size description: Up to 30 mm in length. Associated distribution: Recorded from the West Coast of South Africa. Further distribution uncertain. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Filograna implexa (coral/Lacy tube worm) from the species identification page. PDF page 133, printed page 130. Associated diagnostic features: The key characteristic of Filograna implexa is its intricate tube structure (photos). Tiny worm, grows 4-5 mm in length and 0.5 mm diameter, usually withdraws into the tube matrix on disturbance. Known for forming three-dimensional colonies up to 300 mm in size on reefs, bryozoans, corals, shells and even on sand substrate. Singular, unbranched tubes made of calcium carbonate, fused to form three-dimensional structure providing microhabitat for many other small marine species. Associated colour description: Tube: white calcareous, grey to brown in colour if old. Worm: pink/orange body with white/translucent tentacles that protrude when in situ but are seldom seen. Associated size description: Tube structures can reach 300 mm or larger. Worms 5 x 0.5 mm (seldom seen once disturbed). Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Polychaete worms (Polychaete worms) from the species identification page. PDF page 134, printed page 131. Associated diagnostic features: Polychaetes are segmented bristle worms. They are usually long and thin (but can have oval body shapes), with numerous body segments and fine bristles projecting from many small parapodia (legs). They are identified from several complex features on their head and mouth, which requires microscopic examination. For the purposes of this guide, all long, thin polychaete worms that do not match the previous descriptions can be grouped under the Polychaete sp. FishBoard code ‘PolW’. Associated colour description: Varied. Associated size description: Varied, but usually no more than 150 mm in length and 5 mm in width. Polychaete (PolW) Phylum: Annelida Class: Polychaeta ALL other long, thin bristle/segmented worms can be captured under this category. Common name: Polychaete worms 36 mm 48 mm 18 mm 21 mm 57 mm 14 mm Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Polychaete tubes (only) (Polychaete tubes (only)) from the species identification page. PDF page 135, printed page 132. Associated diagnostic features: Various types of polychaete tubes may be captured in the trawl net. These can include fine, tube-like structures, hard straw-like tubes, parchment-like tubes or thicker skin-like tubes, often covered in mud. Frequently polychaetes may not be visibly present inside these tubes. Please still record the presence of Polychaete tubes and weight using the code PolTub. Associated colour description: Light brown, mud colour. Associated size description: Varied. Polychaete tubes (PolTub) Phylum: Annelida Class: Polychaeta Common name: Polychaete tubes (only) 44 mm 70 mm 90 mm 100 mm Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for ‘Pycnogonid’ - (Common name Sea spider) from the species identification page. PDF page 140, printed page 137. Associated diagnostic features: Pycnogonids (sea spiders) have small bodies with long, spider-like legs. Most have four pairs of jointed walking legs, although some species are known to have five or six pairs of legs. Body form consists of a cephalon and a trunk which has four body segments, each segment bearing a pair of legs. The cephalon bears a proboscis, a pair of chelifores, a pair of palps and a pair of ovigerous legs (ovigers). Ovigers are a feature unique to Pycnogonida. Offshore South African pycnogonids from Iziko Museum, identified by David Staples, are classified into three families: Pallenopsidae, Callipallenidae, and Nymphonidae. However, for purposes of research trawl surveys, all pycnogonids are grouped together under the FishBoard code ‘Pycnog’. Associated colour description: Variable, but usually orange, yellow or red. Associated size description: Variable. From a few millimetres up to 140 mm in diameter (in South Africa). Associated distribution: Ubiquitous in benthic habitats. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Most have four pairs of jointed walking legs, although some species are known to have five or six pairs of legs. Body form consists of a cephalon and a trunk which has four body segments, each segment bearing a pair of legs. The cephalon bears a proboscis, a pair of chelifores, a pair of palps and a pair of ovigerous legs (ovigers). Ovigers are a feature unique to Pycnogonida. Offshore South African pycnogonids from Iziko Museum, identified by David Staples, are classified into three families: Pallenopsidae, Callipallenidae, and Nymphonidae. However, for purposes of research trawl surveys, all pycnogonids are grouped together under the FishBoard code ‘Pycnog’. Associated colour description: Variable, but usually orange, yellow or red. Associated size description: Variable. From a few millimetres up to 140 mm in diameter (in South Africa). Associated distribution: Ubiquitous in benthic habitats. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for ‘Ostracod’ - (Ostracods) from the species identification page. PDF page 141, printed page 138. Associated diagnostic features: Small crustaceans, body completely enclosed in bivalved carapace, hence common name ‘mussel shrimps’ or ‘seed shrimps’. Usually round or oval in outline, most are smooth, but some extravagantly ridged or spiked. Some have conspicuous antennal notch (Order Myodocopa, shown here). Swim using elongate antennae. Can be planktonic or benthic and have various feeding habits, including carnivores, grazers, scavengers and filter-feeders. Associated colour description: Usually white to yellow, sometimes pink/orange organs visible through carapace. Associated size description: Up to 15 mm diameter, mostly much smaller (<5 mm). Associated distribution: Ubiquitous in benthic and pelagic habitats. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for ‘Stalked barnacles’ - (Stalked barnacles) from the species identification page. PDF page 142, printed page 139. Associated diagnostic features: Diverse group of barnacles, most commonly encountered attached to floating objects (‘Goose barnacles’), but in benthic habitats also often found attached to rocks, crustaceans, corals, hydroids, polychaete tubes, etc. Filter-feeding appendages project from laterally-flattened body, which is enclosed in shiny shell plates (plates rarely reduced or even absent in ectoparasitic species). Body characteristically borne on flexible stalk attached to substratum. Stalk may be short or long, and either bare, or armoured with small plates. Associated colour description: Usually white. Associated size description: Typically 2-50 mm tall. Associated distribution: Entire region, surface to abyssal depths. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for ‘Sessile barnacles’ - (Sessile barnacles) from the species identification page. PDF page 143, printed page 140. Associated diagnostic features: Diverse and familiar group of ‘typical’ barnacles, with body completely enclosed in a conical ring of four to eight shell plates. Live permanently attached to rocks, corals, sponges and other benthic substrata (no stalk). Filter-feed using setose appendages projecting from an opening at distal end of shell. Associated colour description: Usually white to pink. Associated size description: Typically 2-50 mm tall. Associated distribution: Entire region, surface to abyssal depths. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for ‘Parasitic barnacles’ - (Parasitic barnacles) from the species identification page. PDF page 144, printed page 141. Associated diagnostic features: Bizarre group of barnacles that parasitise and castrate various species of decapod crustaceans. Body has lost all resemblance to ‘normal’ barnacle and consists of a root-like ‘interna’ penetrating host’s body and an ‘externa’, a bulb-like reproductive body projecting from abdomen or thorax of host. In different species the externa can be a single grape- like structure, or comprise multiple lobes (as shown here on the hermit crab Parapagurus bouvieri) or ‘clubs’. Most species are host-specific. Associated colour description: Usually white or transparent. Associated size description: Externa typically 5-20 mm across. Associated distribution: Whole region, on various crustacean hosts. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pterygosquilla capensis (cape mantis shrimp) from the species identification page. PDF page 145, printed page 142. Associated diagnostic features: Easily recognised by enlarged spearing raptorial claw, which has six to eight teeth and a sharp dactyl. Carapace with central saddle, telson with central keel and six large marginal teeth. The only abundant offshore benthic stomatopod on the West Coast, although several other species are found on the South and East Coasts. Can occur in high densities. Associated colour description: Mostly pale yellow to brown, but can have blue colouration with red and yellow trim in tail portion. Associated size description: Up to 180 mm in length, but usually smaller. Associated distribution: Widespread species. Namibia to southern KwaZulu- Natal. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for ‘Tanaids’ - (Tanaids) from the species identification page. PDF page 146, printed page 143. Associated diagnostic features: Small, cylindrical crustaceans with unstalked eyes. First two thoracic segments fused to head and covered with short carapace, the other six segments remaining separated. First pair of legs bear distinctive strong claws. Filamentous uropods project beyond back end of body. About 20 species occur in region. Associated colour description: Usually white. Associated size description: Can reach 20 mm (as Carpapseudes austroafricanus, depicted), but normally much smaller. Associated distribution: Widespread, in most habitats, especially among sponges, ascidians, etc. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated diagnostic features: Smallish crustaceans, usually with dorso-ventrally flattened bodies, rarely tubular in shape. Two pairs of antennae of very variable length, one pair of unstalked eyes (often large), seven thoracic segments, each with a pair of pereopods (rarely clawed). Over 300 species in the region, with diverse shapes and habits. Some occur as external or as gill and mouth parasites of fish. Associated colour description: Variable, most commonly whitish or brown. Associated size description: Up to 50 mm, but usually smaller (typically 5-20 mm). Associated distribution: Widespread in all habitats. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for ‘Amphipods’ - (Amphipods) from the species identification page. PDF page 148, printed page 145. Associated diagnostic features: Diverse group of small crustaceans, most easily recognised by their laterally compressed bodies. Also characterised by having two pairs of antennae, unstalked eyes, prominent side plates and seven pairs of pereopods, the first two often modified to form conspicuous ‘claws’. Over 300 species occur in the region, occupying almost all habitats and with diverse feeding habits. Abundant in sediments (e.g Ampelisca spp. left), and on reefs, where commonly associated with sponges, seaweeds, ascidians, etc. (e.g. Amaryllis macropthalma, right). Associated colour description: Variable, most often white, but some brightly coloured. Specimens from trawls usually less colourful. Associated size description: Small, most species 5-20 mm. Associated distribution: Ubiquitous, from shore to deep ocean in all habitats. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Jasus lalandii (West coast rock lobster) from the species identification page. PDF page 149, printed page 146. Associated diagnostic features: Carapace with flattened squamous (scale-like) tubercles of various sizes, each pointed with a fringe of setae around the base. Two large spines and a small central rostrum between the eyes. Abdominal segments fringed with setae, the penultimate transverse row better developed than the others, so that a more or less conspicuous groove is formed between it and the hindmost row. Phyllosoma larva transparent and free floating, with flat, leaflike body and long spindly legs. Associated colour description: Reddish brown, often with purplish or violet tints, especially on tail fan, under surface dull yellow, flagellum of antennae often with pale bands. Associated size description: Maximum total body length 460 mm, carapace length up to 180 mm. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. Restricted to southern Africa from Northern Namibia to Algoa Bay. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Palinurus gilchristi (South coast rock lobster) from the species identification page. PDF page 150, printed page 147. Associated diagnostic features: Colour orange with white bands on legs and antennnae. Frontal margin of carapace with 4-6 teeth, outer dorsal processes far apart, splayed outward. Abdominal segments 2-5 with two equally long, deep, hairy grooves on either side of the median keel. The median keel connects the anterior and posterior transverse grooves forming an H-shaped sculpturing. Associated colour description: Orange or reddish, banded with yellow white on abdomen, antennae and legs, pale marks on abdomen mostly at sides and oblique. Associated size description: Between 150-310 mm in length. Associated distribution: South African endemic. South Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Palinurus delagoae (Natal spiny/Deep- sea lobster) from the species identification page. PDF page 151, printed page 148. Associated diagnostic features: Reddish-mauve colour distinctive, large frontal ‘horns’ on carapace widely splayed, carapace less spinose anteriorly, the groups of setae around bases of spines less well-developed, anteriorly almost obsolete. No longitudinal groove on either side of the median keel on abdominal segment 2-5. Anterior groove on abdominal segment 2-5 shorter and less distinct than posterior groove and grooves not linked. Little to no hair on abdomen. Associated colour description: Reddish mauve with irregular ivory white patches, legs and antennae red and white banded. Associated size description: Up to 400 mm in length. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. South and East coasts of South Africa, mainly caught between 100-300 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Projasus parkeri (cape jagged lobster) from the species identification page. PDF page 152, printed page 149. Associated diagnostic features: Highly distinctive, carapace smooth, except for marked submedian and lateral longitudinal series of large spines on either side. Abdomen smooth, a median keel on segments 1-5 and a few spines on segment 6. Associated colour description: Orange or orange-red; flagella of 1st antenna, 5th and 6th joints of legs and membranous part of tail-fan pale in colour. Associated size description: Up to 150 mm in length. Associated distribution: South Coast near East London. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Scyllarides elisabethae (Shovel-nosed/ Slipper lobster) from the species identification page. PDF page 153, printed page 150. Associated diagnostic features: Unmistakable, due to flattened body and short, broad and flattened antennae (used to shovel through sediment). Pereopods distinctively banded vermillion. Antero-lateral corner of carapace sharply produced forwards. Associated colour description: Dull brown, with a rough texture and orange pattern. Associated size description: Up to 250 mm in length. Associated distribution: South Coast, Agulhas Bank to Mozambique. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Homarinus capensis (cape lobster/Pygmy lobster) from the species identification page. PDF page 154, printed page 151. Associated diagnostic features: Resembles a small North Atlantic clawed lobster. Carapace smooth with slight granulation; rostrum short, dorso-ventrally flattened with five to ten small lateral serrations. First three pairs of legs with chelae, those of first pair the largest and subequal. Pereopods 2 and 3 with much smaller chelae. Abdomen elongate and straight, surface slightly pitted, uropods broadly rounded, telson as broad as long, both thickly fringed by setae. Associated colour description: Reddish or reddish-yellow, laterally with longitudinal orange and white stripes. Associated size description: Length up to 100 mm. content: Whole-specimen reference image for Aristaeomorpha foliacea (Giant/royal red prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 155, printed page 152. Associated diagnostic features: Carapace slightly keeled anteriorly. Females with several small teeth on long rostrum, but males with much shorter rostrum. Marked network of lateral ridges on carapace. Chelae on first and third pereopods well developed, eyestalk with tubercle, no postorbital spine. Associated colour description: Deep red-orange. Carapace darker red than abdominal segments. Can be paler red in smaller individuals. Associated size description: Up to 220 mm total length. Associated distribution: Southern Namibia to South Coast of South Africa – demersal species on sandy and muddy bottoms on continental slope at 300-500 m. Widespread in Atlantic and Indo-Pacific and extensively exploited. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Aristaeopsis edwardsiana (Scarlet shrimp) from the species identification page. PDF page 156, printed page 153. Associated diagnostic features: Carapace with dorsal keel extending 70% of carapace length. Rostrum elongate in females and juveniles, shorter in males, with three dorsal and no ventral teeth. Distinct keels on sides of carapace. Abdominal segments dorsally keeled. Pleopods remarkably elongate; first three pairs exceeding length of walking legs. Associated colour description: Variable, ranging from deep crimson to orange. Associated size description: Up to 230 mm total length. Associated distribution: Throughout southern Africa and widespread in Atlantic and Indo-Pacific. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Aristeus varidens (Striped red prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 157, printed page 154. Associated diagnostic features: Males and females have different rostrums. Males have a smaller and shorter rostrum and can have a small 4th tooth. Females have three distinct teeth on dorsal edge near base of rostrum, with a smooth long rostral spine (can curve upwards) and no teeth on ventral margin of spine. Carapace slightly keeled. Associated colour description: Deep red-orange ranging to paler pink in colour in smaller individuals (100 mm). Associated size description: Total length up to 200 mm in females and 120 mm in males. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa and Namibia. Adults at 400–600 m depth, young at 300 m depth on muddy bottoms. Caught mostly at night, suggesting they burrow into substratum by day. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Funchalia woodwardi (Woodward's large pink prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 159, printed page 156. Associated diagnostic features: Carapace with branching lateral keels. Rostrum short, flattened and compact, reaching just past the eye, with 11 dorsal teeth, no ventral teeth, but many fine hairs on ventral surface. Mandibles with elongate scythe-like incisor processes. Pereopods short. Ovaries with unspawned eggs visible through carapace when present. Associated colour description: Pale pink to white, with distinct red to pink bands across tail. Thorax often has darker pink/purple colouration where internal organs are visible. Associated size description: Up to 170 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa and Namibia. Pelagic species, occurring at depths below 550 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Haliporoides triarthrus (Serrated leaf rostrum prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 160, printed page 157. Associated diagnostic features: Easily recognised by large, flattened, curved and serrated leaf-like rostrum with 10 spines on dorsal edge and two spines on ventral edge, no post-rostral keel. Both flagella of antenna 1 much longer than length of animal. Flagellum of antenna 2 also very long. Fourth to sixth abdominal segments keeled, each keel ending in a short spine. Associated colour description: Pale pink. Associated size description: Up to 150 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Solenocera africana (African mud shrimp/ Orange-back prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 161, printed page 158. Associated diagnostic features: Carapace with marked orbital and postorbital spine (just behind eye) and long cervical groove on side. Rostrum short, with seven dorsal spines, none below. Antennal flagella united to form a respiratory tube. Distinguished by bright orange colour along dorsal thorax and tail. Immature individuals between 50-100 m and adults occur in depths of 300 m or more. On sandy and muddy seabeds. Mainly active at night; feed on polycheates, small crustaceans and molluscs. Associated colour description: Golden orange with brighter band along dorsal edge; can also be paler in colour. Associated size description: Up to 140 mm total length. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa through to KwaZulu-Natal, 50-450 m, in sand and mud seabeds. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Sergia spp. (Scarlet prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 162, printed page 159. Associated diagnostic features: Rostrum much reduced, upturned and short, not even reaching eyestalks, with tiny posterior spine. First abdominal segment overlaps second. Ventrally flattened. Anterior part of carapace not elongated beyond insertion of mouth appendages. First pair of pereopods not chelate, second and third pereopods with minute chelae. Associated colour description: Dark red, with carapace deepening in red to black. Associated size description: Up to 125 mm length. Associated distribution: Predominantly West Coast, but can occur along South Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Merhippolyte agulhasensis (Banded-leg red shrimp) from the species identification page. PDF page 163, printed page 160. Associated diagnostic features: Rostrum distinctly serrated on the ventral edge and curves sharply upwards. Five rostral teeth dorsally and five evenly-spaced teeth below. Characteristic red-and-white banded pereopods. Associated colour description: Red bands across tail, red-and-white legs, green eggs in females. Male rostrum’s colour changes from white to red. Associated size description: Male up to 85 mm, female up to 70 mm body length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Parapontophilus gracilis (Orange striped tail/ Golden-eye shrimp) from the species identification page. PDF page 164, printed page 161. Associated diagnostic features: Very small species. Rostrum with short spine not reaching beyond eye, two spines along dorsal margin. Eye glows golden in light. Two spines laterally along carapace. Tail appears banded with mottled pattern. Second pereopod has modified cheliped with expanded hand, palm with strong spine at base. Third pereopod small and slender, remaining pereopods much longer. Associated colour description: Orange-and-white banded, ventral side appears whitish, dorsally orange, with golden eyes. Associated size description: Average 46-50 mm body length. Associated distribution: Global distribution, including West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Philocheras sculptus (Sculpted prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 165, printed page 162. Associated diagnostic features: Carapace with median keel bearing four forward- directed teeth and several smaller keels on lateral margins. Rostrum curved downwards and apically divided into two points when viewed from above. Abdominal segments with dorsal ridges. Associated colour description: Mottled brown and blue when alive, becoming red when preserved. Associated size description: Small, body length up to 20 mm. Associated distribution: South Coast, Algoa Bay to East Coast, Durban. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Glyphocrangon spp. (Armoured shrimps) from the species identification page. PDF page 166, printed page 163. Associated diagnostic features: Robust, rigidly calcified and armoured shrimps of which 10 regional species are described. Rostrum well-developed, dorsally flattened, with upturned tip, laterally spinose, proportionately longer in young than in adult. Carapace strongly sculptured with longitudinal ridges and keel. Abdomen usually sculptured, the segments firmly interlocked. Telson strong, spine-like and pointed, quadrangular in cross section. Eyestalks short, eyes large. Associated colour description: Red. Associated size description: Large; body length up to 110 mm. Associated distribution: Widespread distribution, including the West and South Coasts of South Africa. Tropical Glyphocrangon spp. occur in northern KwaZulu-Natal. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Tropical Glyphocrangon spp. occur in northern KwaZulu-Natal. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Nematocarcinus longirostris (Long-rostrum prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 167, printed page 164. content: Specimen detail reference image for Acanthephyra pelagica (red pelagic prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 168, printed page 165. Associated diagnostic features: Rostrum more than half the length of carapace; seven to nine distinct spines on dorsal margins and five spines on ventral margins. Between 7-11 pairs of lateral spines on telson. Abdominal segments with dorsal keel and posterior spine on segments 3 to 6. No keels present on carapace. Associated colour description: Deep red. Associated size description: Up to 25 mm carapace length (excluding rostrum). Pleon (abdomen) ± 60 mm length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts (> 400 m) of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Notostomus elegans (Dark red double- keeled prawn) from the species identification page. PDF page 169, printed page 166. Associated diagnostic features: Cephalothorax expanded, rostrum curved, strongly serrated on both dorsal and ventral margins, serrations extending along front part of carapace. Distinct lateral carapace keels running along length of carapace. Abdominal segments 3 to 6 with distinct dorsal keels terminating in sharp posterior teeth. Associated colour description: Dark red to black. Associated size description: 60–80 mm total length. Associated distribution: West Coast (> 400 m) of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.
