content: Specimen detail reference image for Crassibougia clausicaudata (Tsitsikamma spindle shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 273, printed page 270. Associated diagnostic features: Shell moderately small, narrowly spindle-shaped, robust when adult; spire whorls sculptured with strong, widely-spaced, rounded axial ribs, these much weaker or scarcely evident on last adult whorl; spiral sculpture of low, flat-topped spiral cords, separated by narrow incised grooves of alternating strength; siphonal canal long with a narrow slit- like opening; aperture of mature specimens with a strong callus nodule just below insertion of outer lip and a well-developed varix behind outer lip. Associated colour description: Shell orange-brown when fresh, the axial ribs usually somewhat paler; aperture whitish. Animal orange- red. Associated size description: Length up to 60 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank (Still Bay to Port Alfred), 50–150 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Fusinus africanae (Africana spindle shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 274, printed page 271. Associated diagnostic features: Shell small, relatively robust, spindle-shaped, with rounded whorls and a strongly indented suture; spire about three-quarters total length of aperture; sculptured by distinct, rather flat, spiral cords and close-set, rounded axial ribs (weaker on body whorl); siphonal canal long, the opening very narrow; protoconch large. Axial ribs almost absent in some individuals. Associated colour description: Shell white to apricot-coloured, usually without further colour pattern; living specimens often thickly encrusted with a brown sponge coating. Associated size description: Adult individuals rarely more than 45 mm in length. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank (Cape Peninsula to Algoa Bay), 100–300 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Fusinus bonaespei (Good hope spindle shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 275, printed page 272. Associated diagnostic features: Shell spindle-shaped with rounded whorls and strongly indented suture; spire equal to, or slightly shorter than, total length of aperture; siphonal canal long and slender; sculpture of narrow spiral cords with intermediary spiral threads; axial sculpture of distinct axial ribs on early spire whorls, but these not evident on later whorls; inner lip without columella pleats; interior of outer lip smooth. Associated colour description: Shell white with pale horn-brown periostracum, frequently flaking off. Animal creamy-white. Associated size description: Length up to 110 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast and Agulhas Bank (Cape Columbine to Algoa Bay), 50–600 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Fusinus hayesi (hayes' spindle shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 276, printed page 273. content: Specimen detail reference image for Fusinus ocelliferus (Spotted spindle shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 277, printed page 274. Associated diagnostic features: Shell narrowly to broadly spindle-shaped; spire half to three-quarters total length of aperture; siphonal canal long and slender (up to one-third total shell length), often somewhat curved; sculpture of coarse, flattened spiral ridges, but strength of sculpture very variable; some specimens with a distinct shoulder bearing rounded nodules; a deep false umbilicus commonly present beside base of siphon in mature specimens; inner lip lacking columella pleats; interior of outer lip smooth. Associated colour description: Shell whitish; spiral ridges frequently spotted or mottled with brown, shoulder when present usually with darker brown spots, particularly on nodules; periostracum horny-brown, somewhat velvety, frequently flaking off. Animal orange-red. Associated size description: Length up to 160 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Namaqualand, West coast to KwaZulu-Natal South coast, infratidal to 150 m, perhaps to 300 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated diagnostic features: Shell narrowly to broadly spindle-shaped; spire half to three-quarters total length of aperture; siphonal canal long and slender (up to one-third total shell length), often somewhat curved; sculpture of coarse, flattened spiral ridges, but strength of sculpture very variable; some specimens with a distinct shoulder bearing rounded nodules; a deep false umbilicus commonly present beside base of siphon in mature specimens; inner lip lacking columella pleats; interior of outer lip smooth. Associated colour description: Shell whitish; spiral ridges frequently spotted or mottled with brown, shoulder when present usually with darker brown spots, particularly on nodules; periostracum horny-brown, somewhat velvety, frequently flaking off. content: Specimen detail reference image for Granulifusus rubrolineatus (red-striped spindle shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 278, printed page 275. Associated diagnostic features: Shell small, broadly spindle-shaped; sculptured with rounded axial ribs crossed by crisp spiral ridges, between which are fine intermediary spiral threads; variable in shell width and strength of axial ribs; siphonal canal relatively short; inner lip not strongly calloused. Associated colour description: Dirty white to pale orange-brown with reddish- brown spiral ridges; some specimens with intervals between axial ribs darker brown; reddish-brown colour of spiral ridges often interrupted where these cross the axial ribs; aperture glossy white. Shell often encrusted with other marine organisms (zoanthids). Associated size description: Length rarely more than 40 mm, often less than 30 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank and East coast, mostly between 100 and 200 m, living on substrata of coarse sand. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Kilburnia heynemanni (Agulhas horse conch) from the species identification page. PDF page 279, printed page 276. Associated diagnostic features: Shell large, broadly spindle-shaped, spire about half total length of aperture; whorls with distinct shoulder bearing strong, widely-spaced nodules; body whorl smooth or spirally ridged; inner lip expanded at base of siphonal canal to form a strong fold, with one to two weaker pleats above this; parietal region with a crisp in-running ridge just below insertion of outer lip; outer lip not sharply drawn in at its base; interior of outer lip smooth. Specimens from shallow water are smaller and have a crenulate outer lip. Associated colour description: Cream to pale orange-brown, with a darker yellowish-brown to dark brown periostracum. Associated size description: Length up to 135 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank (west to False Bay) and Transkei shelf, 25–100 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x159.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Kilburnia scholvieni (cape horse conch) from the species identification page. PDF page 280, printed page 277. Associated diagnostic features: Shell very large, spindle-shaped; spire high, about three quarters total length of aperture; whorls rounded, but often with a weak shoulder bearing low nodules; sculpture of fine spiral threads, some specimens with occasional stronger cords; outer lip sharply drawn in at its base to form a relatively slender siphonal canal; inner lip with strong fold at base of siphonal canal with one to two weak columella pleats above this; parietal region with rounded, in-running ridge just below insertion of outer lip; interior of outer lip mostly smooth, but mature specimens often with subterminal row of denticles behind somewhat flaring outer lip. Associated colour description: Whitish to pale buff or orange brown, nodules often darker brown; periostracum olive-brown to dark brown. Animal orange-red. Associated size description: Length up to 220 mm and perhaps more. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank (Cape Agulhas to Port Grosvenor), 30–250 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x159.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Nassarius speciosus (Shouldered dog- whelk) from the species identification page. PDF page 281, printed page 278. Associated diagnostic features: Shell small, robust, with strong, widely spaced axial ribs crossed by finer, close-set spiral cords; whorls shouldered, ribs rendering shoulder nodular; ribs weaker on last part of body whorl; aperture with distinct siphonal notch; inner lip with well-developed callus extending over columella and parietal region; outer lip with subterminal external thickening and low internal ridges. Associated colour description: Shell whitish to buff, axial ribs paler; aperture and callus white, siphonal notch dark purplish-brown when fresh; surface of living shell usually with a khaki-brown periostracum-like layer of encrusting organisms. Associated size description: Length up to 35 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast to Agulhas Bank (southern Namibia to western Transkei), shallow water to 130 m, possibly deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Nassarius vinctus (Violet-mouthed dog-whelk) from the species identification page. PDF page 282, printed page 279. Associated diagnostic features: Shell small, spire relatively elongated with weakly convex whorls; periphery rounded; sculpture variable, often reticulate, comprising low axial ribs crossed by broad, flat, spiral cords with narrow intervals, but axial ribs sometimes weak or absent; inner lip with thin, glossy callus spreading over parietal region; outer lip not conspicuously thickened, internally smooth or with weak in- running ridges; siphonal notch wide and shallow. Associated colour description: Fresh specimens reddish-brown to purplish-brown, usually with pale spiral bands; axial ribs, if present, paler; inner lip and interior of aperture violet; colour intensity fading with time. Shell frequently encrusted with other marine organisms and surface often chalky or etched. Associated size description: Length up to 22 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast and Agulhas Bank (northern Namibia to western Transkei), 10–150 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated diagnostic features: Shell small, spire relatively elongated with weakly convex whorls; periphery rounded; sculpture variable, often reticulate, comprising low axial ribs crossed by broad, flat, spiral cords with narrow intervals, but axial ribs sometimes weak or absent; inner lip with thin, glossy callus spreading over parietal region; outer lip not conspicuously thickened, internally smooth or with weak in- running ridges; siphonal notch wide and shallow. Associated colour description: Fresh specimens reddish-brown to purplish-brown, usually with pale spiral bands; axial ribs, if present, paler; inner lip and interior of aperture violet; colour intensity fading with time. Shell frequently encrusted with other marine organisms and surface often chalky or etched. Associated size description: Length up to 22 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast and Agulhas Bank (northern Namibia to western Transkei), 10–150 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pteropurpura spp. (Stag shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 283, printed page 280. Associated diagnostic features: Shell small, biconic (two cones); siphonal canal well- developed, with very narrow channel; sculptured by three very strong axial ribs (varices) bearing recurved spines; largest spines at shoulder, decreasing in size on base and siphonal canal. Associated colour description: White to pale brown, some with a pink/orange undertone. Associated size description: Length up to 35 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Continental shelf off the West, South and East coasts, subtidal to 300 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Marginella musica (Musical margin shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 284, printed page 281. Associated diagnostic features: Shell shape and glossy surface typical of Marginella species; striped colour pattern distinctive; adult shells relatively solid, outer lip thickened, lower part of columella with four oblique pleats. Associated colour description: Pale brown to greyish-brown with fine black spiral lines. Animal cream to pale orange, with a pattern of fine red lines on its large foot. Associated size description: Length up to 22 mm. Associated distribution: West coast and Agulhas Bank (Namibia to Algoa Bay), 40–550 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Afrivoluta pringlei (Giant orange margin shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 285, printed page 282. Associated diagnostic features: Shell large, resembling a volute; body whorl oblong, apex bluntly rounded; a well-developed, oval callus deposit adjacent to parietal region; surface smooth and glossy; aperture narrow and elongate; basal half of inner lip with four strong, oblique pleats; outer lip slightly thickened, its edge convex in a side view, internally smooth. Associated colour description: Deep pinkish-orange to orange-brown, body whorl with two or more broad bands of a paler shade; ventral callus cream coloured to pinkish-brown. Associated size description: Length up to 120 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Eastern Agulhas Bank (Knysna area to western Transkei), 70–500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Coluzea radialis (Benguela pagoda shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 286, printed page 283. Associated diagnostic features: Shell relatively thin, siphonal canal long, slender and straight; periphery with a spiral row of bluntly triangular spines (15–50 on last whorl); elsewhere sculptured by spiral cords, most prominent below periphery and on base of siphonal canal; some specimens with low axial ribs associated with peripheral spines. Associated colour description: Shell uniformly white. Associated size description: Length up to 75 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast, off Atlantic Cape region (Alexander Bay to Cape Point), 160–420 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Athleta abyssicola (Yellow-foot hatch shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 288, printed page 285. Associated diagnostic features: Shell moderately elongate, but shell width and spire height variable; aperture long and narrow, comprising well over half shell length; spire conical; sculpture cancellate (hatched), comprising relatively fine axial ribs and spiral cords of more or less equal strength; much of ventral surface with a thin, transparent glaze extending from inner lip; inner lip itself with numerous columella pleats, progressively stronger anteriorly; outer lip slightly reflected, its inner margin thickened and bearing numerous ridge-like denticles. Associated colour description: Surface dull, often etched or eroded; fresh specimens biscuit-coloured to pale orangish- or pinkish-brown; interior of aperture pale apricot, columella pleats white. Surface often encrusted with muddy deposit. Animal greyish-white to yellow, heavily speckled with greyish markings. Associated size description: Length up to 105 mm. Associated distribution: West coast, off Atlantic Cape region (Walvis Bay to Cape Agulhas), 100–550 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Athleta lutosa (Pink-foot hatch shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 289, printed page 286. Associated diagnostic features: Shell relatively broad with a wide aperture; shell thickness very variable; spire conical with convex whorls and indented suture; sculpture less obviously cancellate (hatched), dominated by crisp spiral cords crossed by irregular growth lines; ventral surface with a thin, transparent glaze extending from inner lip; columella with four to six low pleats, sometimes in pairs; outer lip not reflected, its inner margin usually weakly thickened and with indistinct ridges. Lip and callus frequently deformed. Associated colour description: Surface dull, usually etched or eroded; fresh specimens pale cream to apricot-pink, most obvious inside aperture; columella pleats white. Surface often encrusted with muddy deposit or stained reddish-brown. Animal pinkish to mauve, heavily speckled with grey-black markings. Associated size description: Length up to 110 mm, but usually considerably smaller (60–70mm). Associated distribution: West coast, Atlantic Cape (Angola to Saldanha Bay), 20–220 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Fusivoluta pyrrhostoma (Flame-mouthed volute) from the species identification page. PDF page 290, printed page 287. Associated diagnostic features: Shell spindle-shaped, siphonal canal relatively short and dorsally recurved; spire approximately half total length of shell, suture indented; sculpture of low axial ribs, often somewhat curved; base with close- set spiral threads; inner lip and columella smooth; outer lip thin, somewhat flaring, its interior smooth; protoconch (apex) bulbous. Associated colour description: Pale orange-white to light apricot, with thin olive- brown periostracum; surface commonly badly eroded; interior of aperture glossy, deep apricot in fresh specimens, more intense on basal half of inner lip. Associated size description: Length up to 90 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast and western Agulhas Bank (Lambert’s Bay to Mossel Bay), 70–400 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Neptuneopsis gilchristi (gilchristi Gilchrist's volute) from the species identification page. PDF page 291, printed page 288. Associated diagnostic features: Shell large and light, spire high with convex (rounded outward) whorls and indented suture; sculpture of very fine, dense spiral threads; aperture wide, somewhat flaring and tapering to a short siphonal canal; inner lip lacking pleats, but with a thin, smooth callus glaze. Protoconch (apex) bud-shaped, disproportionately large. Operculum smaller than aperture. Associated colour description: Pale buff to pale orange-brown with a thin, persistent lustreless olive-brown periostracum; some specimens with diffuse paler spiral bands. Associated size description: Length up to 240 mm, but usually 120–150 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West and South coast, Agulhas Bank, 60–500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Amalda bullioides (bullioides Bullet amalda) from the species identification page. PDF page 292, printed page 289. Associated diagnostic features: Shell bullet-shaped, smooth and glossy; spire and parietal region enveloped in enamel-like callus, covering sutures; aperture elongate, narrowing apically and with a broad siphonal notch; inner lip concave, outer lip thin. Associated colour description: Fresh shells orange to brown, darkest around suture; body whorl with two narrow white bands separated by a broad orange band; a narrow orange-brown band below lower white band; columella and tip of spire whitish. Old shells much faded. Associated size description: Length up to 42 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast and Agulhas Bank, 100–370 m, possibly deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pulsarella fultoni (fultoni humbug turrid) from the species identification page. PDF page 293, printed page 290. Associated diagnostic features: Shell fairly solid, spire elevated, narrowly tapering to sharp point; outer lip thin, with U-shaped anal sinus just below suture; sculptured with widely spaced spiral cords, one just below apical suture, one at periphery (level with basal suture) and a third, between these, also with several narrower cords on base; intervals between cords concave (hollowed inwards). Associated colour description: Fresh specimens orange-brown to dark brown, spiral cords white; inner lip and base darker purplish- brown. Colour fading in dead specimens. Associated size description: Length up to 32 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Cape Peninsula to Agulhas Bank (from False Bay to western Transkei), 20–85 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated diagnostic features: Shell fairly solid, spire elevated, narrowly tapering to sharp point; outer lip thin, with U-shaped anal sinus just below suture; sculptured with widely spaced spiral cords, one just below apical suture, one at periphery (level with basal suture) and a third, between these, also with several narrower cords on base; intervals between cords concave (hollowed inwards). Associated colour description: Fresh specimens orange-brown to dark brown, spiral cords white; inner lip and base darker purplish- brown. Colour fading in dead specimens. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Comitas saldanhae (saldanhae Benguela comitas) from the species identification page. PDF page 294, printed page 291. Associated diagnostic features: Shell spindle-shaped, with short siphonal canal and elevated spire; whorls shouldered and suture indented; shoulder slope sculptured with spiral threads only, sculpture below shoulder comprising oblique axial ribs crossed by finer spiral threads, base with spiral threads and growth lines only; outer lip with broad, moderately deep, U-shaped anal sinus at shoulder, lip edge flaring outward below this in large specimens. Associated colour description: Shell chalky white, with dull brown periostracum; apex, ribs and subsutural region frequently eroded; often covered in mud. Associated size description: Length up to 62 mm, but usually less than 45 mm. Associated distribution: West coast (Namibia to west of Cape Point), 50–600 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Comitas stolida (stolida Agulhas comitas) from the species identification page. PDF page 295, printed page 292. Associated diagnostic features: Shell spindle-shaped, with elevated spire; whorls angled at periphery and with distinct, obliquely elongate nodules, somewhat rib-like; shell otherwise sculptured only by growth lines and close-set, microscopic, spiral threads; outer lip with moderately deep, U-shaped anal sinus below suture, lip edge convex below this. Associated colour description: Brown to reddish-brown, peripheral nodules whitish. Associated size description: Length up to 55 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. South coast, Agulhas Bank, 60–150 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Conus gradatulus (gradatulus Agulhas cone shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 296, printed page 293. Associated diagnostic features: Shell light in weight, body whorl weakly convex, angular at shoulder; spire broadly tapering to a sharp tip, but spire height variable; spire with stepped profile due to angular shoulder; whorls concave above shoulder, essentially smooth; base of body whorl with weak spiral threads, otherwise sculpture comprising only weak growth lines; aperture elongate and narrow, outer lip thin. Operculum very small, oblong-ovate. Associated colour description: Ground colour white, variously marked with orange- brown or reddish-brown, often in a broad spiral band below shoulder, commonly broken up to form wavy axial stripes, sometimes almost covering whole body whorl; shoulder slope and spire white with occasional orange-brown axial flames. Living specimens with a thin, translucent, olive-yellow periostracum, partially obscuring underlying shell colour pattern; West Coast specimens generally uniformly whitish, lacking colour pattern and often chalky (form patens). Associated size description: Length up to 80 mm. Associated distribution: From Namibia (Walvis Bay) and West Coast to Agulhas Bank, 30–500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Philine aperta (headshield/Shelled aperta sand slug) from the species identification page. PDF page 297, printed page 294. Associated diagnostic features: Shell internal, entirely covered by body of animal; body divided into a head shield (flattened for burrowing in sandy substrata), a posterior shield (overlying viscera and internal shell) and two lateral lobes, one on each side. Internal shell thin and translucent. Associated colour description: Animal uniformly milky white to yellowish, somewhat translucent. Associated size description: Adult body length 60–70 mm, up to 100 mm. Associated distribution: Saldanha Bay, West coast to Mozambique, subtidal to 100 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x161.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Scaphander punctostriatus (punctostri- Giant canoe bubble atus) from the species identification page. PDF page 298, printed page 295. Associated diagnostic features: Shell elongate and rather bubble-like, thin and fragile; no spire evident, body whorl expanding rapidly so as to cover earlier whorls; smooth but under a microscope sculptured by fine spiral lines of tiny elongate pits (punctations); aperture elongate, very broad basally. Animal large, cannot retract completely into shell. Associated colour description: Shell whitish with a thin yellowish periostracum, sometimes with faint, darker spiral bands. Animal yellowish-white. Associated size description: Length 30–40 mm. Associated distribution: Outer continental shelf and upper slope, West coast and Agulhas Bank, 170–2700 m (also much of the North Atlantic, Gulf of Mexico and Mediterranean). Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pleurobranchaea bubala (bubala Warty pleurobranch) from the species identification page. PDF page 300, printed page 297. Associated diagnostic features: Honeycomb, mottled colouration of brown/black/ yellow on dorsal surface. Very soft, fleshy body with a slimy surface texture. If left in water, two rhinophores (chemosensory tentacles) located dorso-laterally often appear and a tube-like mouth. Branchia (feather-like gills) are clearly visible from the ventral view on the right side of the animal, as is the foot. Pleurobranchaea has a very soft body that does not retain shape well out of water. Associated colour description: Mottled brown/yellow/black colouration on dorsal surface, which often wears off on the most elevated areas to be translucent. Ventral body cream to white. Associated size description: Average 60 to 70 mm. Associated distribution: West coast, South coast to Port Elizabeth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Leminda millecra (millecra Frilled nudibranch) from the species identification page. PDF page 310, printed page 307. Associated diagnostic features: White-edged mantle relatively thin with large sinuous folds. Anterior break in mantle edge between the rhinophores (chemosensory tentacles). Large oral veil. Rhinophores pale, smooth and tapering, and do not retract into a pocket. Digestive gland divided into relatively fine ramifying ducts, which can be seen through the translucent body wall. Colour dependent on food colour in digestive gland ducts, but varies between pink and brown. Associated colour description: Pink to brown with an opaque white dorsal edge. Highly variable, depending on the food in the digestive ducts. Associated size description: Up to 90 mm. Associated distribution: West coast of Cape Peninsula to Kwa-Zulu Natal, South coast, in 10–104 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Nucula nucleus (nucleus common nut clam) from the species identification page. PDF page 311, printed page 308. Associated diagnostic features: Shell roundly triangular, but not equilateral (have unequal sides), posterior slope longer than anterior one; surface sculptured with somewhat irregular concentric growth lines (often scarcely evident) and microscopic radial lines; ventral margin finely denticulate; hinge with comb-like dentition (taxodont – with numerous fine interdigitating teeth). Associated colour description: Whitish with a dull olive-brown periostracum; often encrusted with pale orange or reddish deposits. Associated size description: Length up to 13.5 mm. Associated distribution: South coast, Agulhas Bank (from False Bay to eastern Transkei), 40–350 m. Also in western Europe and Mediterranean. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated colour description: Milky-white to yellowish-white, somewhat glossy; dorsal and ventral edges usually with marginal band of khaki-brown periostracum. Associated size description: Length up to 40 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. South coast, Agulhas Bank (from False Bay to western Transkei), 30–500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Solemya togata (Mediterranean togata awning clam) from the species identification page. PDF page 313, printed page 310. Associated diagnostic features: Shell very fragile, gaping at each end, with a thick, horny periostracum that projects well beyond ventral and anterior margins like an awning; anterior region of shell with broad low ridges, evident also in periostracum; hinge essentially toothless. Foot of living animal long, visible at anterior end, the tip truncated, ending in a disc with a fringed margin. Associated colour description: Shell translucent white to buff, periostracum glossy, initially honey-brown, becoming dark brown with growth. Associated size description: Shell length up to 40 mm. Associated distribution: West Coast, Saldanha Bay to Mossel Bay, 30–250 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Limopsis chuni (chuni cape limopsis) from the species identification page. PDF page 314, printed page 311. Associated diagnostic features: Shell almost circular in outline, usually covered throughout with dense, fine periostracal hairs, but these sometimes partially or entirely worn off; underlying shell sculptured with fine concentric ridges and indistinct radial lines; hinge with comb- like dentition (taxodont – with numerous fine interdigitating teeth). Associated colour description: Shell whitish, periostracal hairs light brown; often coated in mud. Associated size description: Length up to 40 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast and Agulhas Bank, 50–430 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Atrina squamifera (squamifera Scaly horse-mussel) from the species identification page. PDF page 315, printed page 312. Associated diagnostic features: Shell triangular in shape, large and fragile; hinge line straight, broad (posterior) end rounded and gaping; surface sculptured by six to twelve rounded ribs radiating from pointed anterior end; ribs bearing well-developed, curved (vaulted) scales, particularly in posterior half; strength of sculpture variable; living specimens with a ‘beard’ of long byssal threads projecting from antero-ventral region. Associated colour description: Light greyish-brown to horn-brown, semi-translucent, darkening with age. Associated size description: Length reportedly up to 390 mm, but rarely more than 250 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Saldanha Bay to East London; commonly found in lagoons and estuaries, but also occurs on the Agulhas Bank at depths of 30–120 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Ostrea atherstonei (atherstonei cape brooding oyster) from the species identification page. PDF page 316, printed page 313. Associated diagnostic features: A typical oyster with a large, flat shell; somewhat rounded in outline; lower valve shallow, without a recess below hinge; externally with coarse overlapping growth lamellae. Associated colour description: Purplish brown to wine red occasionally with dark rays; interior whitish, often pink edged. Associated size description: Maximum diameter 105 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West coast Saldanha Bay to KwaZulu-Natal, South coast, shallow subtidal reefs. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pecten sulcicostatus (Agulhas ridged sulcicostatus scallop) from the species identification page. PDF page 317, printed page 314. Associated diagnostic features: Shell large, right valve convex, left valve flat and slightly smaller than the right one; ears of equal size; sculptured by 12-15 radial ribs. On the right valve the ribs have sloping sides and are wider than their intervals, while the whole surface bears fine secondary radial threads; left valve with higher, more flat-topped ribs, no wider than their intervals, which lack secondary radial threads. Associated colour description: Cream to buff, left valve usually mottled with pink, salmon, or pinkish-brown, right valve paler, although often tinged with pink or salmon towards umbo (adults generally very pale); interior white. Associated size description: Maximum diameter 106 mm, usually 60–80 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank (from False Bay to East London), 30–70 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Pseudamussium gilchristi (gilchristi Gilchrist's scallop) from the species identification page. PDF page 318, printed page 315. Associated diagnostic features: Shell typically scallop-shaped, but with ± 8 low, broad, rounded radial ribs and sculptured all over with fine, granulose radial riblets; ears of unequal size. content: Specimen detail reference image for Lucinoma capensis (capensis cape lucina) from the species identification page. PDF page 319, printed page 316. Associated diagnostic features: Small to medium-sized; shell outline almost circular; umbones more or less central and curved slightly forward; valves of equal size; sculptured by thin, raised, concentric ridges, often eroded at umbones; hinge with two cardinal teeth per valve; interior pallial line without sinus; ventral margin smooth. Associated colour description: Shell white, with thin horn-brown periostracum when fresh; usually coated in mud. Associated size description: Diameter up to 40 mm. Associated distribution: West coast to South coast; Namibia to Transkei shelf, 30–450 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Schizodentalium plurifissuratum (plurifissura- Multi-fissured tusk tum shell) from the species identification page. PDF page 322, printed page 319. Associated diagnostic features: Shell resembles a miniature elephant’s tusk; no evidence of coiling; shell hollow, tapering from one end to the other, slightly curved; sculptured with fine, close-set, longitudinal ridges; narrow end (posterior) with a row of one to five longitudinal, slit-like perforations on convex surface (occasionally none). Associated colour description: Shell dirty white to yellowish-cream; frequently stained with blackish marks. Associated size description: Length up to 70 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Agulhas Bank (from False Bay to western Transkei), 70–300 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Leptochiton sykesi (sykesi Sykes's chiton) from the species identification page. PDF page 323, printed page 320. Associated diagnostic features: Animal with eight valves (plates) covering dorsal surface, surrounded by a thin girdle with fine velvety spicules; valves strongly arched and mid- line of animal angular; valve surface with numerous extremely fine longitudinal beaded threads (only visible under a microscope), lateral areas of valves two to seven weakly raised and with concentric growth lines. Associated colour description: Valves whitish, usually stained to varying degrees with black (sometimes heavily so); girdle yellowish- white to pale apricot. Associated size description: Length up to 23 mm. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Known only from off the south-western Cape (from Saldanha Bay to Cape Point), 70–433 m, but mostly deeper than 250 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Argonauta argo (Greater argonaut) from the species identification page. PDF page 329, printed page 326. Associated diagnostic features: • Head small, embedded within mantle. Eyes prominent, protruding and constricted at base. • Ink sac present. All arms with two rows of suckers. Webs between arms shallow. • : Arms I thick at base, length variable, large membranous flap extending full length of arm. Arms IV more than 3x ML and 20-30% longer than Arms II. Arm formula IV > II > III. • : Small. 12-13 suckers on normal arms. • Lateral ribs smooth, continuous or branched from axis to keel, aligned with keel tubercles. • Dorsal keel narrow and constant width around circumference of shell. • Keel tubercles consistent in size and arranged in pairs with a ridge across keel between pairs. hectocotylus Left Arm III. Long, slender, self-amputating extension (almost as long as the arm) kept coiled in sac below left eye. Associated size description: Females attain 97 mm ML, 300 mm shell length. Males 9 mm ML. Associated distribution: Circumglobal between 40° N and 40° S. Pelagic, surface to 200 m on both West and South Coasts. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Argonauta hians (Lesser argonaut) from the species identification page. PDF page 330, printed page 327. Associated diagnostic features: • Head small, embedded within mantle. Eyes prominent, protruding and constricted at base. • Ink sac present. All arms with two rows of suckers. Webs between arms shallow. • : Arms I thick at base, length variable, large membranous flap extending the full length of arm. Arms II & III 1.4x to 2x ML and 20-50% longer than Arms IV. Arm formula III ≥ II > IV. • : Small. 10-11 suckers on normal arms. • Lateral ribs prominent smooth without tubercles, not aligned with keel tubercles. • Dorsal keel width and tubercle size increase with growth (i.e. from apex towards aperture). Tubercles alternate on either side of keel. • Dorsal keel wide. hectocotylus Left Arm III. Long, slender, self-amputating extension (almost as long as the arm) kept coiled in sac below left eye. content: Distribution map for Argonauta nodosus (Knobbed argonaut) from the species identification page. PDF page 331, printed page 328. Associated diagnostic features: • Head small, embedded within mantle. Eyes prominent, protruding and constricted at base. • Ink sac present. All arms with two rows of suckers. Webs between arms shallow. • : Arms I thick at base, length variable, large membranous flap extending the whole length. Arms II 2.0 to 2.8 times ML; Arms III & IV subequal. Arm formula II > III ≈ IV. • : Small. 17-20 suckers on normal arms. • Lateral ribs ending in a chain of separate tubercles terminating in an acute keel tubercle. • Dorsal keel width and tubercle size increase with growth (i.e. from apex towards aperture). Tubercles alternate on either side of keel. hectocotylus Left Arm III. Long, slender, self-amputating extension (almost as long as the arm) kept coiled in sac below left eye. Associated size description: Females attain 138 mm ML, 292 mm shell length. Males 11 mm ML. Associated distribution: Circumglobal in southern hemisphere between 10° S and 44° S. Pelagic, surface to 200 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Bathypolypus valdiviae (Deepwater octopus) from the species identification page. PDF page 332, printed page 329. Associated diagnostic features: • Ink sac absent. • Small, smooth, purplish octopod with ovoid muscular mantle. • Head narrower than body, eyes slightly protuberant. • Interbranchial web pouches absent. • A single papilla over each eye. • Arms short, subequal with two rows of small suckers, webbed for 33% of length. Arm formula I ≈ II ≈ III ≈ IV. hectocotylus Right Arm III. Ligula a broad, rounded disc with a deep trough bearing four big transverse laminae. Associated size description: 80 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both coasts, but more common on West Coast. Generally 450 to 1000 m depth, but has been recorded at 200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Benthoctopus berryi from the species identification page. PDF page 333, printed page 330. Associated diagnostic features: • Ink sac absent. • Buccal area and ventral surface of arms chocolate brown. • Suckers large, prominent, arranged in two alternating rows, i.e. not arranged in pairs. • Arms three to six times longer than mantle. Arms I, II and III subequal in length and longer than Arms IV. Arm formula: I ≈ II ≈ III > IV. hectocotylus Right Arm III. Ligula short, narrow 5-7% of hectocotylised arm length. Associated size description: 50 mm ML. Associated distribution: Rare endemic. West Coast from 600-2 200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Enteroctopus magnificus (Southern giant octopus) from the species identification page. PDF page 334, printed page 331. Associated diagnostic features: • Ink sac present. • Large and robust, without enlarged suckers on arms. • Arm length moderate 3.5-5.0 times ML; subequal in length. Arm formula II = I > III = IV. • Colour usually with reddish tones; distinctive fold of loose skin at the end of the mantle. • No large erectile papillae on dorsal mantle; single large papilla and three or four cirri over each eye. hectocotylus Right Arm III. Ligula long (16-25% of length of arm), tapering to a blunt terminus. Associated size description: Up to 360 mm mantle length and more than 10 kg. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts. Usually deeper than 100 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Octopus vulgaris (common octopus) from the species identification page. PDF page 335, printed page 332. Associated diagnostic features: • Ink sac present. • Large muscular species; arms long, 4x to 5.5x ML; lateral pairs distinctly longer than median pairs; Arm formula III ≥ II > IV > I. • Both sexes with two to three enlarged suckers on lateral arms at level of 15th–19th proximal suckers. • Colour usually with greyish tones. No loose skin fold at the end of the mantle. • Four large erectile papillae in diamond arrangement on dorsal mantle. • One to two supraocular papillae over each eye. hectocotylus Right Arm III. Ligula small, spoon-shaped, 2.5% of arm length. Associated size description: Maximum weight 10 kg. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts. To about 200 m, but generally less than 100 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Opisthoteuthis massyae (Umbrella octopus) from the species identification page. PDF page 336, printed page 333. Associated diagnostic features: • Arms almost fully encased in a thick web with a single row of suckers to the tips, flanked by a row of cirri on either side. A pair of small fins near posterior end of mantle. • It looks like a dark reddish-brown gelatinous blob, and it is only the eight rows of suckers on the oral side that show that it is a cephalopod. • In males, the proximal four suckers on each arm are small, the next three to six enlarged, then decrease progressively to tips, but with a second field of enlarged suckers at the web margin. • Dorsal arms (Arms I) of males thick, muscular and robust to web margin, distal 3rd attenuate and slender. Dorsal arms of females not different to the other arms. hectocotylus None. Associated size description: 70 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts between 500 and 1 500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Vampyroteuthis infernalis (Vampire squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 337, printed page 334. Associated diagnostic features: • Eight arms, webbed for most of their length. • Two long filamentous limbs that can be retracted into pits between Arms I and II. • A single row of stalked suckers on distal 2/3 of arms, flanked by a row of cirri on either side. • Body gelatinous with black pigmentation. • A pair of small fins near posterior end of mantle in adults; juveniles with two pairs of fins. hectocotylus None. Associated size description: Maximum 130 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Mesopelagic (600-1 200 m) on West and South Coasts. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Spirula spirula (ram’s horn squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 338, printed page 335. Associated diagnostic features: • Tightly coiled, chambered shell. The shell is internal, but visible on both dorsal and ventral surfaces. • Rectangular mantle with small fins at the posterior corners. • Mantle margin produced dorsally and deeply emarginated ventrally. • Colour dark reddish brown, but usually skinned during trawl capture. Club Small, marginally wider than the stalk. Suckers small, subequal. hectocotylus Both ventral arms modified. Associated size description: Maximum size 45 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Pelagic in surface waters on West and South Coasts. Seldom captured on demersal surveys, regular on pelagic surveys. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia angulata from the species identification page. PDF page 340, printed page 337. Associated diagnostic features: • Mantle short and broad. Dorsal margin convex, not produced. Ventrally entire. • Fins narrow, rounded. Separate posteriorly. • Dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms densely covered with large, coarse papillae. • Ventral surface of mantle generally smooth, with widely scattered large papillae. • Arm suckers arranged in four series to tips. • Arms I to III long and strongly attenuated. Club Long, slightly recurved, bearing numerous subequal small suckers. hectocotylus Not described. Associated size description: ML up to 100 mm ( ) and 120 mm ( ). Associated distribution: Coastal to 350 m on South Coast, but all research survey records 100-110 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia australis (Southern cuttlefish) from the species identification page. PDF page 341, printed page 338. Associated diagnostic features: • Strong, robust spine on posterior end of cuttlebone. • Mantle oval, dark purple dorsally. Ventral surface reddish-brown to orange when fresh, but on death fades to white with red centre. • Mantle margin produced dorsally and straight (not emarginated) ventrally. • Suckers on arms in four rows. Club Short, somewhat recurved. Suckers arranged in transverse rows, five suckers per row; size varies markedly, smaller distally and four greatly enlarged median suckers near proximal end. hectocotylus Left ventral arm hectocotylised. Associated size description: Up to 85 mm mantle length, 5 gram. Associated distribution: Common on both West and South Coasts to 500 m, but most abundant (90% of records) 60-200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia dubia from the species identification page. PDF page 342, printed page 339. Associated diagnostic features: • A fleshy ridge without pores on sides of ventral mantle typical of the subgenus Hemisepius. • Shell hard, well-calcified, unlike any others in the subgenus Hemisepius. • Mantle rounded, papillate, with two large complex wart-like growths. • Mantle margin straight dorsally, deeply emarginate ventrally. • Wide keels on ventral arms. • Arms with small suckers to the tips. Suckers biserial, but widely spaced so that they look as though they are uniserial. Club Small, with few small subequal suckers. hectocotylus Not described. Only known specimens are female. Associated size description: 17 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Very rare, known from only two specimens, 150-200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia faurei from the species identification page. PDF page 343, printed page 340. Associated diagnostic features: • A fleshy ridge on sides of ventral mantle typical of the subgenus Hemisepius visible as a narrow iridescent blue line without pores. • Shell thin, not calcified, but middle hard to the touch as in most Hemisepius. • Mantle broad, almost round. Dorsal margin straight, deeply emarginate ventrally. • Dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms brownish, densely covered with small round papillae. • Arm suckers small, globose and biserial. Arms I attenuated for distal half, tips finger-like, devoid of suckers. • Web between Arms I, II and III not reaching half of arm length. Club Broad and slightly recurved with 33 small suckers in transverse rows. Median suckers slightly larger than lateral suckers. hectocotylus Not described. Associated size description: Most small (20-30 mm ML), but specimens of over 40 mm ML have been recorded. Associated distribution: South Coast; from coast to 900 m. Rare and easily overlooked. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia hieronis from the species identification page. PDF page 344, printed page 341. Associated diagnostic features: • Dorsal projection of mantle in males long, reaching to between eyes, shorter in females. • Ventral mantle margin entire in males, emarginated in females. • Fins a very narrow, inconspicuous fringe along mantle, separate posteriorly. • Dorsal arms shorter than ventral arms, with suckers to the tips. • Suckers biserial on basal two-thirds of arms. Proximal 3rd biserial (females) or quadriserial (males). • Dorsal colour reddish brown. Ventral colour white, with reddish or orange border near base of fins. No posterior spine. Club Small, curved with five to six transverse rows of numerous small subequal suckers. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Modified region about half of arm. Transversely wrinkled with minute lateral suckers. Associated size description: 80 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, between 40 and 550 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia papillata from the species identification page. PDF page 345, printed page 342. Associated diagnostic features: • Mantle broadly oval. Mantle margin dorsally produced, ventrally slightly emarginated. • Fins wide, rounded. Separate posteriorly. • Dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms densely covered with small tubercles. • Wrinkled areas on ventral surface of mantle and on outer sides of ventral arms. • Arm suckers not globose, arranged in four series basally, in four ( ) or eight ( ) rows on distal ¼. • Arms I to III not attenuated, webbed for about half of length. • Colour: Dorsal dark reddish-brown to purple; ventral pale with scattered chromatophores. Club Long. Small suckers distally. Four enlarged medial suckers. Middle two extremely large, diameter approximately equal to width of the sucker-bearing surface of the club. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. In modified region sucker rows two and three separated by naked area with transverse ridges. Associated size description: 140 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Mainly South Coast shallower than 210 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Sepia robsoni from the species identification page. PDF page 346, printed page 343. Associated diagnostic features: • A fleshy ridge on sides of ventral mantle typical of the Subgenus Hemisepius visible as a narrow iridescent line without pores. • Shell thin, completely chitinous, lacking the hard centre of other Hemisepius. • Mantle broad; dorsal margin convex, almost straight; ventral margin deeply emarginate. • Dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms brown, covered with small, round papillae. • Arm suckers small, globose and biserial. Distal half of dorsal arms finger-like, devoid of suckers. Suckers to the tips of ventral and dorso-lateral arms. • Arms I, II & III webbed half of arm length. • Wide fin not reaching edge of mantle (along 60-80% of mantle) and separate posteriorly. Club Crescent-shaped, with about 53 subequal suckers in transverse rows of four to six. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Ten pairs of minute suckers in modified basal ¾. Associated size description: Maximum 20 mm. Associated distribution: Uncommon on both West and South Coasts, from 300-500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 155x205.

content: Distribution map for Sepia simoniana from the species identification page. PDF page 347, printed page 344. Associated diagnostic features: • Mantle broadly oval. Mantle margin dorsally produced, ventrally entire. • Fins narrow, rounded. Separate posteriorly. • Skin finely papillose on dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms. • Wrinkled areas on outer sides of ventral arms and rarely on ventral mantle. • All except ventral arms attenuated over distal quarter. • Arm suckers not globose, quadriserial to tips of all arms in both sexes. • Colour: Dorsal pinkish-brown; ventral pale with scattered chromatophores. Club Very long, more than half the length of mantle, with numerous minute suckers. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Modified region – two ventral and two dorsal rows of minute suckers separated by naked region with transverse ridges. Distal half normal. Associated size description: 185 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Mainly South Coast. Recorded to 190 m, but usually less than 100 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia sp. A from the species identification page. PDF page 348, printed page 345. Associated diagnostic features: • A fleshy ridge on sides of ventral mantle typical of the subgenus Hemisepius visible as a narrow iridescent blue line without pores. • Shell reduced. Middle hard to the touch, margins soft, chitinous. • Mantle margin: slightly convex dorsally; ventrally entire or shallowly emarginate. • Arm suckers small and biserial. Dorsal arms not attenuated and bearing suckers to the tips. • Dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms greenish, with well-spaced round papillae. Club Small, crescent-shaped, thicker than tentacle, with numerous small subequal suckers. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Associated size description: Up to 17 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, between 50 and 500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia tuberculata from the species identification page. PDF page 349, printed page 346. Associated diagnostic features: • Mantle short and broad. Dorsal margin convex, not produced. Ventrally entire. • Fins wide, rounded. Separate posteriorly. • Dorsal surface of mantle, head and arms densely covered with large coarse tubercles. • Large wrinkled patches on either side of otherwise smooth ventral surface of mantle and on outer area of ventral arms. • Arm suckers not globose, arranged in four series to tips. Tips of Arms I to III attenuated, webbed for less than half of arm length. Club Long, slightly recurved. Small suckers distally with enlarged suckers proximally. Diameter of largest suckers less than width of the sucker-bearing part of the club. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. The two dorsal rows of suckers normal, separated from reduced ventral suckers by a broad naked area with transverse ridges. Distal half of arm normal. Associated size description: 82 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Shallower than 200 m on South Coast. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia typica cf. from the species identification page. PDF page 350, printed page 347. Associated diagnostic features: • A fleshy ridge on sides of ventral mantle typical of the Subgenus Hemisepius, with 5-15 (usually 10-12) diagnostic obvious black pores. • Shell not calcified, very thin and fragile, but hard to the touch. • Mantle very broadly oval, almost as wide as long; dorsal margin convex; ventral margin entire or emarginated. • Dorsal surface of head and mantle greenish, sparsely papillose. • Suckers globose, biserial and extending to the tips of the arms. Tips not attenuated. • Arms short, subequal in length; interbranchial web between Arms I-III half arm length. • Fins narrow, fused posteriorly. Club Small and straight, with numerous small subequal suckers in transverse rows of six. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Basal half modified. Suckers minute, widely spaced, separated by fleshy transverse ridges. Associated size description: 25 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts, from coast to 600 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Sepia vermiculata from the species identification page. PDF page 351, printed page 348. Associated diagnostic features: • Mantle broadly oval. Dorsal margin convex, somewhat produced, ventral margin entire (male) or emarginated (female). • Fin wide, rounded. Along entire margin of mantle with small gap at tail. • Skin smooth, both dorsally and ventrally, no obvious pores or wrinkled patches. • Ventral arms longest, dorsal arms shortest. Arms III and IV keeled, joined by shallow web. • Suckers on arms in four rows, extending to somewhat attenuated tips. • Some individuals show diagnostic transverse zebra-like stripes on mantle and ventral arms. Club Large, one third of mantle length; distal suckers small in oblique rows of eight; proximal suckers in oblique rows of five, with median suckers 1.5-2 times and middle suckers 3 times the size of the marginal suckers. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Modified region with 9-12 rows of reduced suckers separated by transverse ridges. Associated size description: 287 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Mainly shallow water on South Coast, but recorded to 290 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

Club Large, one third of mantle length; distal suckers small in oblique rows of eight; proximal suckers in oblique rows of five, with median suckers 1.5-2 times and middle suckers 3 times the size of the marginal suckers. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Associated distribution: Mainly shallow water on South Coast, but recorded to 290 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Sepia vermiculata from the species identification page. PDF page 351, printed page 348. Associated diagnostic features: • Mantle broadly oval. Dorsal margin convex, somewhat produced, ventral margin entire (male) or emarginated (female). • Fin wide, rounded. Along entire margin of mantle with small gap at tail. • Skin smooth, both dorsally and ventrally, no obvious pores or wrinkled patches. • Ventral arms longest, dorsal arms shortest. Arms III and IV keeled, joined by shallow web. • Suckers on arms in four rows, extending to somewhat attenuated tips. • Some individuals show diagnostic transverse zebra-like stripes on mantle and ventral arms. Club Large, one third of mantle length; distal suckers small in oblique rows of eight; proximal suckers in oblique rows of five, with median suckers 1.5-2 times and middle suckers 3 times the size of the marginal suckers. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Modified region with 9-12 rows of reduced suckers separated by transverse ridges. Associated size description: 287 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Mainly shallow water on South Coast, but recorded to 290 m. Extracted image dimensions: 138x208.

content: Distribution map for Austrorossia enigmatica (Bobtail squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 352, printed page 349. Associated diagnostic features: • Shell rudimentary, chitinous, feels as though there is no internal shell. • Fins large, rounded, attached about midway along mantle; broadly separated posteriorly. • Anterior mantle edge not fused with head dorsally, not covering funnel ventrally. • Head short and broad, constricted round crown of circumoral appendages anterior to eyes. • Eyes large, prominent iridescent green. Club Narrow, not wider than tentacle. Suckers microscopic in 30-40 rows. hectocotylus Both dorsal arms. Associated size description: 40 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West (common) and South (uncommon) Coasts, between 200 and 500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Inioteuthis capensis from the species identification page. PDF page 353, printed page 350. Associated diagnostic features: • Shell absent. • Fins small, rounded, attached mid-laterally to mantle. Broadly separated posteriorly. • Mantle fused with head dorsally by a narrow occipital band. • Funnel not covered by a forward extension of the anterio-ventral edge of mantle. • Body oblong, longer, less eyeball-like than Stoloteuthis. • Ventral surface of ink sac without luminous organ. Club Small, slightly wider than stalk, with small suckers. hectocotylus Left dorsal arm. Basal part modified into specialised copulatory apparatus. Associated size description: 20 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Common in surface waters on both coasts, but seldom recorded on demersal surveys because of small size. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Stoloteuthis (Eye-ball squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 354, printed page 351. Associated diagnostic features: • Shell absent. • Fins large, ear-like, attached laterally to posterior half of mantle. Broadly separated posteriorly. • Dorsal mantle edge fused to head by a broad occipital band 40-50% of head width. • Anterior edge of mantle extended as a ventral shield to level with eyes. • Body round, globular, looks like an eyeball. Top of head iridescent green. • First three pairs of arms joined by a deep web. Suckers on arms in two series. • A luminous organ on ventral side of ink sac. Club Not thicker than tentacle, with numerous small suckers. hectocotylus Both Arms II. Associated size description: 20 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Between 100 and 850 m, mainly on West Coast. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Afrololigo mercatoris (African thumbstall squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 355, printed page 352. Associated diagnostic features: • Lens of eye covered by a cornea, not in direct contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage a simple straight groove and ridge. • Arms with two rows of suckers, clubs with four rows. No hooks. • Dorsal arms (Arms I) much shorter than other arms. • Fins translucent, short (40% ML) and rounded, with convex posterior margins. • White, with irregular reddish-brown spots on mantle, head and arms. Club Narrow, small, with suckers arranged in four longitudinal rows; four to five pairs of medial suckers on manus enlarged, sucker rings with 15-25 teeth. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Basal half normal; distal half with elongate papillae. Associated size description: Males 50 mm mantle length, females 35 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts to 470 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Loligo reynaudii (chokka squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 356, printed page 353. Associated diagnostic features: • Lens of eye covered by a cornea, not in direct contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage a simple straight groove and ridge. • Arms with two rows of suckers, clubs with four rows. No hooks. • Mantle narrow, firm and elongate. • Skin red, deciduous, usually rubbed off in the trawl. • Fins posterior, long, over 65% of mantle, rhomboidal in shape, with concave posterior margin. Club Tentacles long; clubs expanded; suckers in four series; suckers on manus greatly enlarged, cups smooth without chitinous teeth. hectocotylus Left ventral arm. Basal part of arm with two series of suckers. Suckers on distal part reduced, but with elongated stalks to form papillae making a feathery tip. Associated size description: Males up to 400 mm mantle length. Females smaller. Associated distribution: Widespread on both coasts but most common on South Coast, shallower than 200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Chtenopteryx sicula (comb-finned squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 357, printed page 354. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage a simple straight groove and ridge. • Mantle broadly rounded with fins along full length, similar shape to cuttlefish. • Fins comprised of slender rib-like muscle bundles connected by membranes, giving a comb-like appearance. • Arms I to III with suckers in transverse series of 6 to 14; Arms IV with a few small suckers in a zigzag pattern. • Large photogenic patches on ventral surface of eyeballs. • Minute suckers on lappets of the buccal membrane. Club Narrow, not expanded, with minute suckers in 8 to 20 irregular transverse series. hectocotylus None. Associated size description: Up to 100 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts, from 500 to 1000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ancistrocheirus lesueurii (Sharpear enope squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 358, printed page 355. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage a simple straight groove and ridge. • Fin very large, rhomboidal, length 70-80% and width 80% of ML. Attached from anterior of mantle ending slightly subterminal. Dorsal surface with irregular brownish transverse dashed lines (photophores). • Posterior end of mantle elongated, forming a tail. • Arms robust with two series of hooks. Small suckers sometimes present on tips. • Ventral surface of mantle studded with 20-24 relatively large separated photophores. No photophores on eyeballs or viscera. Club Tentacles robust, 12 photophores on aboral side of stalk. Clubs not expanded, two series of hooks on manus, no suckers. Discrete carpal cluster. hectocotylus Right ventral arm. Associated size description: Attains 410 mm mantle length, and 3 kg. Associated distribution: Mesopelagic and bathypelagic on West and South Coasts. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Brachioteuthis picta (Ornate arm squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 359, printed page 356. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage a simple straight groove and ridge. • Mantle long and slender; slightly flared at opening; abruptly narrows anterior to fins. • Fin almost as wide as long, length and width about 50% ML. • A sausage-shaped photophore on ventral surface of each eye. • Skin smooth in both sexes, never rough even in mature individuals. Club Manus greatly expanded, covered with numerous rows of small, long-stalked suckers; dactylus section with three to four rows of suckers. hectocotylus Not described. content: Distribution map for Chiroteuthis (Atlantic long-arm mega squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 361, printed page 358. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage oval, with two knobs directed towards centre of the concavity. • Head and neck long and tubular, but squarish near the eyes; head plus neck almost as long as mantle; eyes about midway between arm bases and mantle. • Fins thick and fleshy, together circular, not lobed; gladius extends as a short tail past fins. • Arms IV much longer and thicker than other arms; Arms II and III subequal; Arms I short. • Eyeball with one photophore on anterior margin just above midline and one just below midline on posterior margin; a series of seven photophores on anterio-ventral margin. • A single photophore on the ink sac. Club Tentacles long and very thin; club fringed with lappets; suckers in four rows arranged in pairs at the base of each lappet; a long oval light organ at the tip of the club. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 100-200 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, from 700 to 1400 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Cranchia (scabra rough cranch squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 362, printed page 359. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle fused to posterior-lateral corners of funnel, no funnel-locking cartilage. • Head fused to the mantle at the nuchal cartilage. • Ventral surface of mantle with two cartilaginous strips extending posteriorly from anterior apex of each funnel-mantle fusion; funnel fused to head laterally. • Brachial photophore on end of each arm in mature females. • Eyes with 14 small round photophores: an inner group of six around pupil; an outer group of seven in an arc on ventral edge; one between the inner and outer groups. • Mantle a thin-walled sac covered in spiky, cartilaginous tubercles. • Fins small (less than 25% ML) posterior. Each nearly oval with free posterior lobe. Club Not expanded, with small sub-equal suckers. Alternating series of carpal suckers and pads for most of tentacle length. hectocotylus Right ventral arm. Suckers in four series on midpoint of hectocotylised arm. Associated size description: Up to 150 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts, from 400 to 1200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Leachia (cyclura Leach’s cranch squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 363, printed page 360. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle fused to posterior-lateral corners of funnel, no funnel-locking cartilage. • Head fused to the mantle at the nuchal cartilage. Funnel fused to head laterally. • Mantle spindle-shaped, tapering to sharp point, with a cartilaginous dorsal keel. • Ventral surface of mantle with one cartilaginous strip extending posteriorly for 20-30% of mantle length from anterior apex of each funnel-mantle fusion. • Body translucent with scattered chromatophores and three dark internal organs easily visible. • Large elongate brachial photophore on tips of Arms III in mature females. • Eight eye photophores, five in outer row and three near pupil. • Fins terminal, rounded and fused posteriorly. Club Median suckers on manus greatly enlarged. hectocotylus Not described. Associated size description: Maximum 150-200 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: South Coast to west of Cape Point, from surface to 2 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Liocranchia (reinhardt’s cranch reinhardti squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 364, printed page 361. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle fused to posterior-lateral corners of funnel, no funnel-locking cartilage. • Mantle spindle-shaped, tapering to sharp point; cartilaginous tubercles along dorsal midline. • Head fused to the mantle at the nuchal cartilage. Funnel fused to head laterally. • Ventral surface of mantle with a pair of cartilaginous strips, studded with tubercles, extending posteriorly in a V-shape from apex of each lateral funnel-mantle fusion (four strips in total). • Mature females with brachial photophores on tips of Arms III only. • 14 small photophores around eyes: four around pupil; eight in ventral arc; two between the two series. • Fins terminal, rounded and fused posteriorly. hectocotylus Right or left ventral arm. Suckers in two series on midpoint of hectocotylised arms. Club Slightly expanded with small, sub-equal suckers. An alternating series of carpal suckers and pads for most of tentacle length. Associated size description: Maximum 250 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Pelagic to mesopelagic on West and South Coasts. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Megalocranchia (maxima Large cranch squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 366, printed page 363. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle fused to posterior-lateral corners of funnel, no funnel-locking cartilage. • Head fused to the mantle at the nuchal cartilage; funnel free from the head laterally. • Mantle slender, elongate, tapers dramatically to thin sharp tip, lacking cartilaginous tubercles. • Most of ventral hemisphere of eyes covered by two photophores, a large crescent-shaped posterior photophore and a smaller anterior photophore within its concavity. • Large, complex, bilobed compound photophore present on ventral surface of rounded digestive gland and ink sac. • Long lanceolate fins (50% ML) terminal-lateral without anterior lobes; anterior 10-15% of fin fused to lateral margins of mantle (unique to Megalocranchia and Teuthowenia). Club Carpal suckers in two series on tentacular stalk; clubs moderate, slightly expanded with suckers in four series. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 1 800 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, 600-2000 m during day; migrates to 100-700 m at night. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Taonius (pavo Peacock cranch squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 368, printed page 365. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle fused to posterior-lateral corners of funnel, no funnel-locking cartilage. • Head fused to the mantle at the nuchal cartilage. • Funnel free from the head laterally. • Mantle without cartilaginous tubercles, very long, slender, tapering to long thin “tail”. • Head small; eyes large, bulbous with one large posterior crescent-shaped photophore that engulfs the small anterior photophore. • Arms with biserial, spherical suckers; without hooks. • Fins long (50% ML), narrow, lanceolate, very attenuated posteriorly. Anterior lobes small. Club Moderate without hooks; enlarged suckers with one or two large hook-like teeth. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 650 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts. Juveniles below 600 m, adults to 2000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Teuthowenia (pellucida) from the species identification page. PDF page 369, printed page 366. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle fused to posterior-lateral corners of funnel, no funnel-locking cartilage. • Head fused to the mantle at the nuchal cartilage. • Mantle thin, leathery; funnel free from the head laterally. • Funnel/mantle fusion cartilages small, oval with one to four cartilaginous tubercles at mantle margin. • Head small; eyes large, bulbous, with three nested photophores – a large crescent-shaped posterior photophore, within its concavity a smaller crescent-shaped anterior photophore and a third small oval photophore. • Brachial end-organ (photophore) on tips of Arms I-IV of mature females. • Fins long, narrow, terminal-lateral, taper posteriorly, terminating in small lobes that extend posteriorly beyond the tip. Club Tentacles short, muscular; carpal suckers in four series in a zigzag pattern on stalk; club slightly expanded with suckers on long pedestals. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 210 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts. Occur at greater depths with age; juveniles and subadults to 1 000 m; adults 1 000-2 500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Discoteuthis (discus Discus squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 370, printed page 367. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Fin large, disc-like, wider than long, equal to mantle length. • Suckers on arms globose, biserial. • A single photophore on ventral mantle near posterior end of body; no photophores on head or near anterior edge of mantle, or on ink sac. • Funnel-locking cartilage triangular, with an oblique groove. Club Compact, widened, with four rows of suckers, two central rows greatly enlarged, globose. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 600 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Rare. Possible on both West and South Coasts, 500 to 1000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Abraliopsis (Micrabralia) (gilchristi Gilchrist’s enope squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 371, printed page 368. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage a simple straight groove and ridge. • Characteristic purplish buccal area contrasting with the whitish bases of the arms. • Mantle, head and arms covered with small photophores visible as small spots. • Photophores on ventral surface of head arranged in five to seven clearly defined longitudinal lines, no photophores between these lines. • Arms IV without suckers, two to four (usually three) large photophores covered by black chromatophores on tips of arms. • Eyeball with five photophores ventrally, anterior and posterior photophores enlarged. • Fin strongly emarginated, lacking posterior lobes and not extending past end of mantle. Club Two series of hooks and one series of suckers on manus. hectocotylus Right or left Arm IV. Associated size description: 40 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Mainly northern parts of West Coast, 200 to 1400 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Histioteuthis (Ornate/Bonnelli’s bonnellii jewel squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 373, printed page 370. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Body covered with minute photophores. • Left eye much larger than right. • Mantle short, subequal to head length; no cartilaginous tubercles on mantle or arms. • Arms joined by an inner web to 50% or more of arm length. • Tip of each of Arms I–III bearing a single large elongate photophore. • Buccal membrane with six lappets (see image on page 369); 17 (rarely 16 or 18) photophores around right eye. • Large compound photophores on ventral surface of head and on Arms III and IV. Club Small, with four to eight rows of suckers of varying sizes. hectocotylus Both dorsal arms. Associated size description: Up to 330 mm mantle length (largest Histioteuthis). Associated distribution: Mainly on West Coast, from 500 to 1500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.
