content: Distribution map for Histioteuthis (meleagroteuthis crested jewel squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 375, printed page 372. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Body covered with minute photophores. • Left eye much larger than right. • Mantle short, subequal to head length; inner web between arms less than 15% of arm length. • No large elongate photophores at the tips of arms. • Buccal membrane with seven lappets; 19-21 photophores around right eye. • Eight to nine longitudinal rows of photophores in basal parts of Arms IV; 30 transverse rows of photophores on ventral mantle. • Large cartilaginous tubercles on dorsal midline of mantle and basal parts of Arms I–III. Club Small, with four to eight rows of suckers of varying sizes. hectocotylus Both dorsal arms. Associated size description: Up to 114 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West Coast. Off the shelf in water column to over 1 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

Club Manus with closely packed suckers of varying sizes in six to seven series. hectocotylus Both dorsal arms. Associated size description: Up to 270 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Most common Histioteuthis species in the region, on both coasts in 700 to 900 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Histioteuthis (reversa reverse jewel squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 377, printed page 374. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Body covered with minute photophores. • Left eye much larger than right. • Mantle elongate, much longer than head, lacking cartilaginous tubercles on dorsal midline. • Arms robust, of moderate length, without cartilaginous tubercles or terminal photophores. • Inner web between Arms I-III low, vestigial. • Buccal membrane with seven lappets; 18 photophores around right eye. content: Distribution map for Joubiniteuthis (portieri Joubin’s squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 378, printed page 375. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage oval, without knobs. • Arms I-III very long, more than 2x ML, with very small suckers in six series. • Arms IV short (1/3 length of other arms), with suckers in four series. • Head narrow, eyes small without photophores. • Mantle long and narrow. • Fin round, short (30% ML); long thin tail (longer than mantle). Club Long and laterally compressed; minute suckers in 5-12 transverse series; no carpus. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 105 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: West Coast, very rare. Meso- to bathypelagic from 500 m to over 3 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Lycoteuthis (lorigera crowned firefly squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 379, printed page 376. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Large luminous organs in body cavity visible through the ventral mantle: Two round photophores near mantle opening; five round photophores in a transverse row across mantle anterior to edge of fin; a luminous cross bar near the posterior end of the mantle. • Ventral side of eyeball with five luminous organs arranged in a single row. • No hooks present. Suckers in two series on arms and four series on clubs. • Males: Arms II greatly elongated, with a series of regularly spaced photophores; Arms III elongated, strongly attenuated. • Muscular, conical mantle. • Fins broad, rhomboidal. Club Four rows of suckers. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: Males 190 mm ML. Females 110 mm. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts. Deeper than 300 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Mastigopsis (hjorti hjort’s whiplash squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 380, printed page 377. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage oval without knobs. • Mantle weakly muscular, semi-gelatinous. • Two photophores on ventral surface of eyeball; no other photophores on eye or body surface. • Arm suckers biserial, no hooks; ventral arms thicker than other arms, greatly elongated. • Fins very large, diamond-shaped, reaching almost to anterior edge of mantle (about 90% of ML); width greater than ML. Club Tentacles vermiform, extremely long, slender; club elongate, with numerous minute suckers arranged in more than 15 series. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 100 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts. Oceanic pelagic or benthopelagic. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Octopoteuthis (rüppell’s octopus sicula squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 381, printed page 378. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Arms robust, with biserial hooks enveloped in soft integumentary sheaths. Minute suckers only at tips. • Small, black, spindle-shaped terminal photophore at the tips of each arm. • One pair of photophores embedded in posterior mantle; and three pairs on lateral sides of funnel groove near neck. • Fin large, length ca 90% ML, width ca 115% ML. Club Tentacles present in paralarval stage (up to 15 mm ML) only, absent in adults. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 200 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts. Meso- to bathypelagic down to about 2 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Taningia (danae Taning’s octopus squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 382, printed page 379. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Arms robust, with large hooks in two series to tips. Minute suckers sometimes at tips. • Tips of Arms II with large, oval, swollen photophore. No photophores on other arms. • No photophores embedded in mantle or arms. One photophore on either side of intestine ventral to the ink sac. • Fin very large, length ca 100% ML, width ca 130% ML. Club Tentacles present in paralarval stage (up to 45 mm ML) only, absent in adults. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 1 700 mm mantle length, 161 kg. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts. Meso- to bathypelagic down to about 2 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ommastrephes (bartramii Neon flying squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 383, printed page 380. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel groove: Foveola with five to eight longitudinal folds; side pockets two to five (usually three to four), distinct, but can be difficult to see on fresh, wet specimens. • No ocular, intestinal or large dorsal photophores; small scattered subcutaneous photophores embedded in the mantle, head and ventral arms (not easily visible). • Arms strong, not attenuated, bearing biserial suckers; swimming keels well-developed. • In adult females, the ventral membranes of Arms III expand into large, triangular lobes. • Colour red, dorsal surfaces typically darker than ventral; a long, wide, silvery or golden opalescent strip on ventral midline from mantle opening to the level of the fins. • Fins terminal, large, rhomboidal, slightly attenuated posteriorly; length 40-50% ML; width 60-85% ML; shorter and wider than Todarodes. Club Dactylus with four rows of small suckers. Manus with enlarged suckers, largest suckers with four large pointed teeth (one in each quadrant). Carpal- locking apparatus present. hectocotylus Right or left ventral arm, smooth without suckers. Associated size description: ( ) 400 mm ML; ( ) 900 mm ML. Associated distribution: Oceanic, offshore of the 200-m isobath where sea surface temperature is 10-25 ˚C. Surface to 1 500 m, but not close to seabed. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

PDF page 383, printed page 380. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel groove: Foveola with five to eight longitudinal folds; side pockets two to five (usually three to four), distinct, but can be difficult to see on fresh, wet specimens. • No ocular, intestinal or large dorsal photophores; small scattered subcutaneous photophores embedded in the mantle, head and ventral arms (not easily visible). • Arms strong, not attenuated, bearing biserial suckers; swimming keels well-developed. • In adult females, the ventral membranes of Arms III expand into large, triangular lobes. • Colour red, dorsal surfaces typically darker than ventral; a long, wide, silvery or golden opalescent strip on ventral midline from mantle opening to the level of the fins. • Fins terminal, large, rhomboidal, slightly attenuated posteriorly; length 40-50% ML; width 60-85% ML; shorter and wider than Todarodes. Club Dactylus with four rows of small suckers. Manus with enlarged suckers, largest suckers with four large pointed teeth (one in each quadrant). Carpal- locking apparatus present. hectocotylus Right or left ventral arm, smooth without suckers. Associated size description: ( ) 400 mm ML; ( ) 900 mm ML. Associated distribution: Oceanic, offshore of the 200-m isobath where sea surface temperature is 10-25 ˚C. Surface to 1 500 m, but not close to seabed. Extracted image dimensions: 287x155.

content: Distribution map for Ornithoteuthis (Bird squids) from the species identification page. PDF page 384, printed page 381. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle cylindrical and muscular, narrow, extending posteriorly as a long thin tail; head broad, equal to or broader than mantle width. • Fins long, sharply lanceolate, posterior margins concave accentuating the long tail. • Funnel groove: Foveola with 7-12 folds; side pockets obscure few or none. • No external or subcutaneous photophores; two visceral photophores: one large, round, yellowish near the anus, other small, oval, white at posterior end of intestine, pinkish bioluminescent strip extends from the small photophore to posterior tip of mantle cavity. • A single round photophore patch on ventral surface of each eye. • Arms strong with well-developed swimming keels; suckers biserial with toothed rings. Club Suckers in four series. Medial manus suckers very large, with 18-20 equal-sized teeth: carpal-locking apparatus weakly developed, lacking knobs. hectocotylus Right Arm IV. Structure differs between species (see next page). Associated size description: 300 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both West and South Coasts, surface to 1 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ornithoteuthis antillarum; Ornithoteuthis volatilis (antillarum Atlantic bird squid volatilis) from the species identification page. PDF page 385, printed page 382. Associated diagnostic features: Phylum: Mollusca Cephalopoda 382 Ornithoteuthis antillarum Atlantic bird squid (OrnAnt) hectocotylus Ventro-lateral view of right Arm IV (figure reproduced from Jereb & Roper, 2010, with permission) showing: Distal half with sucker stalks modified into papillae; honeycomb sculpturing along midventral surface consisting of four or five longitudinal columns of depressions and swollen ridges. There are 20 to 25 depressions (pits or pores) in each column. distribution Known global distribution North Atlantic south to at least 28° S off Namibia. Possible off the northern West Coast. Ornithoteuthis volatilis Shiny bird squid (OrnVol) hectocotylus Oral and ventro-lateral views of right Arm IV (figure reproduced from Jereb & Roper, 2010, with permission) showing: Distal half with sucker stalks modified into papillae; honeycomb sculpturing along midventral surface consisting of two or three longitudinal columns of depressions and swollen ridges. There are 10 to 15 depressions (pits or pores) in each column. distribution Known global distribution Indo-West Pacific to east coast of Africa. Reported from the Benguela off South Africa and Namibia. Possible off both West and South Coasts. Ornithoteuthis Males Phylum: Mollusca Class: Cephalopoda Order: Oegopsida Suborder: - Family: Ommastrephidae Common: Alternate: Atlantic and Shiny bird squids - Ornithoteuthis volatilis MALE: DORSAL VIEW Males usually skinned in the trawl Hectocotylised right Arm IV Head as wide as or broader than mantle opening Fin and mantle extend as long tail 50 mm Associated distribution: O. antillarum: North Atlantic south to at least 28 degrees S off Namibia. O. volatilis: Indo-West Pacific to east Africa and the Benguela region. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Todarodes (Shiny bird squid angolensis Angola flying squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 386, printed page 383. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle cylindrical and muscular, narrow and tight in , wider and looser in . • Trawl-caught males usually skinned. • No light organs on eyes, viscera or mantle. • Arms strong, with well-developed swimming keels. Suckers biserial, with toothed rings. • Funnel groove with foveola (containing longitudinal folds) only, side pockets absent. • Fin large; convex anterior margin; posterior margin attenuated to form short tail. Club Very long; manus with 14-18 quadriserial sucker rows, medial manus suckers enlarged, with 13-16 long pointed teeth. Four pairs of carpal suckers. hectocotylus Right Arm IV long, with suckerless thick pedicels forming a feather-like fringe for distal 40% of arm. Associated size description: 430 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both South and West Coasts. Offshore of the 300 m isobath. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Todarodes (filippovae Antarctic flying squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 387, printed page 384. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle cylindrical and muscular, narrow. • No light organs on eyes, viscera or mantle. • Arms strong, with well-developed swimming keels; suckers biserial, with toothed rings. • Funnel groove with foveola only, side pockets absent. • Fin large; convex anterior margin; posterior margin attenuated to form short tail. Club Short, well-developed; manus wide with 12-14 quadriserial sucker rows; medial manus suckers enlarged, with 7-13 long pointed teeth; carpus very short, only two pairs of carpal suckers. hectocotylus Right Arm IV long, with suckerless thick pedicels forming a feather-like fringe for distal 21-36% of arm. Associated size description: Max female 540 mm, male 400 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Circumpolar south of 35° S. Rare on South Coast. Oceanic 300-1 200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Todaropsis (eblanae Lesser flying squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 388, printed page 385. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle robust, stout, but thinner walled and flabbier than Todarodes, especially in . • Head broad, with four nuchal folds on neck; funnel groove without foveola or side pockets. • Arms strong, with well-developed swimming keels. Suckers biserial with toothed rings. • Largest arm suckers with one large median tooth and three or four smaller teeth. • No light organs on eyes, viscera or mantle. • Fin large, broad, width about twice length, anterior edge convex. Club Dactylus with four rows of small suckers. Manus with six transverse rows of four suckers, medial suckers 4x larger than lateral suckers. Largest suckers with about 30 teeth. hectocotylus Bases of both ventral arms with beak-like lappets, edges brown in mature . Associated size description: 290 mm mantle length in females; 220 mm for males. Associated distribution: Both South and West Coasts, 20-850 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Notonykia (Benguela clubhook africanae squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 389, printed page 386. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle densely muscular, broad. Skin smooth, not rugose. Photophores absent. • Nuchal folds four to five on each side of neck. • V-shaped funnel groove without fleshy ridge. • Arms robust, subequal (33-55% ML) with biserial suckers. • Colour maroon to brick red, darker dorsally. • Fin large, rhomboidal 58-66% ML, anterior margins slightly convex, posterior almost straight. Club Dactylus 20-38 minute suckers; manus narrow, two medial series of 14-20 (usually 17-18) strong hooks, no marginal suckers; carpus well defined, 6-12 smooth suckers plus knobs. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 180 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Common on West Coast. Bathypelagic to 1 200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Onychoteuthis (common clubhook banksii squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 390, printed page 387. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle very robust, densely muscular. • Skin smooth, without warts or wrinkles. • Nine to ten pairs of prominent, elongate, flap- like nuchal folds dorso-laterally on neck. • A large bi-lobed, patch-like light organ on ventral surface of each eye. • Two large bulbous visceral photophores on ventral midline, posterior 2x size of anterior. • Fins moderate, rhomboidal, sharply pointed posteriorly. Club Dactylus with 13-15 small suckers in four series. Manus slightly expanded with 20-22 large strong hooks in two medial series; no marginal suckers. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 300 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Possible on both South and West Coasts. Epipelagic, usually in surface 150 m, but has been recorded to 4 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Onykia (robsoni Warty squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 391, printed page 388. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle robust, long and slender. • Skin rugose, covered with flat, irregular warts. • Photophores absent on mantle, eyes and viscera; no nuchal folds. • Arms attenuated with two series of suckers; Arms IV longest. • Fins heart-shaped, very long, attenuated, drawn into long lanceolate tail. Club Manus long, slender, not expanded, 26-32 hooks in two medial series. No marginal suckers. Minute suckers on dactylus. Carpus clearly defined. hectocotylus Absent. Associated size description: 900 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Both South and West Coasts in deep waters, 500 to 2 500 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Pyroteuthis (margaritifera Jewel enope squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 392, printed page 389. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Mantle without embedded photophores, cone- shaped. Head broader than mantle. • Arms I-IV long and strong, armed with hooks in two series almost to tips. • Arms without photophores. • Ventral surface of eye with 12 photophores, nine large and three small. • Ten photophores in mantle cavity, three in transverse row at level of the gills. • Six to seven separated photophores embedded in tentacular stalk. • Fins semi-circular, subterminal. Club Manus with a central series of three to five hooks and two series of suckers. hectocotylus Right ventral arm, without tooth plate. Longitudinal membrane along 33% of arm. Associated size description: 50 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: South and West Coasts. Mesopelagic 400-800 m during the day, migrating to upper 200 m at night. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Thysanoteuthis (rhombus rhombic squid) from the species identification page. PDF page 393, printed page 390. Associated diagnostic features: • Eye not covered by a transparent membrane, lens in open contact with seawater. • Funnel-locking cartilage diagnostic, vertical groove that is joined by a transverse groove about halfway along its length in a -shape. • Mantle very muscular and powerful, bluntly rounded. • Arms short, strong, biserial suckers, no hooks. • Well-developed protective membranes on long cirri-like structures on all arms, but most obvious on Arms III. • Arms I-III with distinct aboral keels. • Young squid (60-350 mm ML) with a well- developed photophore on ink sac. Reduced, non-functional in adults. • Rhomboidal, muscular fin 100% of mantle length, widest 1/3 from front. Club Tentacles relatively short, strong. Clubs long, widened with four series of suckers. Carpal-locking apparatus a series of alternating knobs and suckers on stalk proximal to clubs. hectocotylus Left ventral arm (IV). Distal third modified. Small untoothed suckers. Associated size description: 1 300 mm mantle length. Associated distribution: Off the continental shelf (offshore of the 400 m isobath) on both South and West Coasts. Pelagic, usually found at or near the surface. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Coronaster volsellatus (False brisingid/Spiny pom-pom starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 401, printed page 398. Associated diagnostic features: Characterised by having a small, circular disc, sharply differentiated from long, slender, slimy and usually deciduous arms (arms readily fall off, look for parts in catch!), always more than five arms, usually up to 11 arms. Arms and body surface covered by sharp spines, each with a tuft or “pom pom” of pedicellariae. Tube feet suckered in two rows. Skeleton is a delicate mesh, often reduced to scattered plates. Brisingid species are unlikely to be whole when landed in a trawl net, any parts should be recorded. Associated colour description: Orange and white patterning, salmon coloured to red. Associated size description: Usually ± 110 mm radius, i.e. 220 mm arm tip to arm tip (diameter), but recorded up to 630 mm diameter. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa. Depth from 250-300 m and likely deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cosmasterias felipes (Indistinct star) from the species identification page. PDF page 402, printed page 399. Associated diagnostic features: Plates on upper surface in regular longitudinal rows, arm tips paler in colour, distinct madreporite located off-centre. Coarse texture. Arms usually readily detach from centre disc once out of water. Four rows of tube feet evident, characteristic of all Asteriidae family. Associated colour description: Brown, pink to orange, with pale tips of arms. Associated size description: Up to 100 mm diameter, but frequently smaller. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Depth from 79-373 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

PDF page 402, printed page 399. Associated diagnostic features: Plates on upper surface in regular longitudinal rows, arm tips paler in colour, distinct madreporite located off-centre. Coarse texture. Arms usually readily detach from centre disc once out of water. Four rows of tube feet evident, characteristic of all Asteriidae family. Associated colour description: Brown, pink to orange, with pale tips of arms. Associated size description: Up to 100 mm diameter, but frequently smaller. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Depth from 79-373 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Marthasterias africana (African spiny starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 403, printed page 400. Associated diagnostic features: One row of distinct, solid spines projecting all along midradius (carina) of each arm. Other aboral spines also present. Spines have rosettes of pedicellariae encircling spines. Small disc with long, chunky arms. Four rows of tube feet, each with a sucker disc. Five long, tapering arms. Marginal plates inconspicuous. Has tiny red dot on tip of each arm. Legs break off quite easily with handling. Four rows of tube feet evident, characteristic of all Asteriidae family. Associated colour description: Brick red to orange or blue-grey with spines mostly orange in colour. Tips of arms usually deeper maroon colour. Associated size description: Up to 180 mm radius sampled. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa; depth from 50 to 150 m, possibly deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Sclerasterias spp. (Small spiny starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 404, printed page 401. Associated diagnostic features: Main radius of each arm has an array of distinct spines along the arm which are smaller in size than those of Marthasterias africana, but are more numerous in Sclerasterias spp. This species is generally smaller in size and has a more slender body shape. The midradial spine (carina) is not as large or distinct as that of Marthasterias africana. Sclerasterias species usually have distinct brown to red to purple colouration. Four rows of tube feet evident, characteristic of all Asteriidae family. Associated colour description: Brick red to orange/brown, with white spines. Associated size description: Up to 60 mm diameter. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa, but seldom encountered. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Perissasterias polyacantha (Very large orange star) from the species identification page. PDF page 405, printed page 402. Associated diagnostic features: Very large in size, arms usually break off easily or are broken off on disturbance. Can have five to seven arms. Marginal plates inconspicuous, tips of arms often curl. Four rows of tube feet, sharp spines lining rows of tube feet. Aboral surface (adambulacral plates) has middle ridge of spines (carina) distinctly enlarged and tipped white that are visibly larger and thicker than other spines. Six rows of spines either side of aboral spine ridge. Madreporite located nearer to arm than to disc centre. Associated colour description: Bright orange, with distinct white-tipped spines along midradial ridge. Associated size description: Average 200-300 mm radius from tips of legs if present. Up to 620 mm arm tip to arm tip, 70 mm disc, 280 mm arm length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Depth 96 to 760 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Anseropoda grandis (Pancake/Goosefoot star) from the species identification page. PDF page 406, printed page 403. Associated diagnostic features: Large in size (up to 300 mm diameter), flat and thin, flexible, but tears easily. Two rows of tube feet. Each arm has raised midradial ridge running the length of the arm. Shape described as a ‘maple leaf-like’. Species is fragile and often breaks up easily in the trawl. Please keep a look out for fragments and record. Associated colour description: Orange. Associated size description: Up to 300 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, up to Port Elizabeth. Depth from ± 275 to 315 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Callopatiria granifera (Red starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 407, printed page 404. Associated diagnostic features: Thick finger-like, blunt-tipped arms, almost semi- circular in cross-section. Granular texture on aboral surface said to resemble overlapping tiles. Associated colour description: Variable, some can be bright red to deep orange, or ranging to pale with darker patches. Usually has a lighter, paler shade on oral surface. Associated size description: Can reach up to 150 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. Known to occur on West and South Coasts of South Africa, usually in shallow water to ± 90 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Callopatiria formosa (Purple starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 408, printed page 405. Associated diagnostic features: Thick finger-like, blunt-tipped arms (some more than others), almost semi-circular in cross-section. Granular texture on aboral surface resembles overlapping tiles. Distal plates on arm tips are more enlarged and rounded than in Callopatiria granifera. Associated colour description: Blue-grey, purple to red, pale purple centrally grading to pale orange distally, underside white. Associated size description: Up to 80 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa. Previously only reported from False Bay, South Africa, 12-55 m depth. Verify identification and depth distribution needed. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus (Astropecten orange trim) from the species identification page. PDF page 409, printed page 406. Associated diagnostic features: Distinct marginal plates separated by grooves on aboral and oral sides. Lower marginal plates project beyond upper plates to form a distinct edge to disc and arms. Both series of marginal plates bear spines. Tube feet in two rows. Node in centre of disc sometimes raised (anal cone). Disc plates (paxillae) fine, often darker brown in colour, sometimes with distinct line down centre of each arm. Plates on upper surface with clusters of short spinelets. Madreporite in a slightly depressed area near marginal plate. Associated colour description: Pale orange to apricot/pink marginal plates, with darker pink/brown/mauve body. Distinct darker brown/purple lines along central aboral side of each arm. Often brighter orange bands separate each marginal plate. Pale cream colouring on oral side. Associated size description: Up to 90 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Common on both West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 50 m to +200 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Astropecten cingulatus (Shallow water Astropecten) from the species identification page. PDF page 410, printed page 407. Associated diagnostic features: Has relatively short, petaloid (petal-like) arms and distinct marginal plates on both aboral and oral surfaces with distinctly elongated oral marginal plates. Lower marginal plates project beyond upper plates to form a distinct edge to disc and arms. A deeper mid-line colouration can be evident on the aboral disc plates (paxillae). Both series of marginal plates bear spines. Tube feet in two rows. Sometimes node raised in centre of disc (anal cone). Associated colour description: Dusty pink to brown/purple colouring on upper surface. The spines protruding from the marginal plate may be dark purple-brown but pale towards the tips. Pale cream colouring on oral side. Associated size description: Up to 90 mm diameter. Associated distribution: This is a shallow-water species found more commonly on the South Coast of South Africa, from 0-65 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Astropecten exilis (Long-arm Astropecten) from the species identification page. PDF page 411, printed page 408. Associated diagnostic features: Small disc; long, narrow tapering arms, flexible. Fine- grained aboral (top) plates, papillae-like. Distinct marginal plates on both aboral and oral sides. Three long spines on outer edge of oral marginal plate. Two rows of tube feet ending in a point, but without sucker disc. Associated colour description: Light brown in colour, marginal plates paler in colour. Associated size description: 150 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Previously recorded off Natal, however trawl specimens found along West and South Coasts of South Africa. Depth from 180 m to ±250 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Dipsacaster sladeni capensis (Coarse-grained orange star) from the species identification page. PDF page 412, printed page 409. Associated diagnostic features: A common deep-water starfish found off South Africa. Distinct, large, star-shaped body form (stellate). Arms form triangle shape with body, ranging ~70-100 mm in diameter. Relatively large disc, coarse body texture. Arms tapering and pointed. Madreporite covered over by paxillae. Paxillae in regular rows. Tube feet are pointed. Marginal plates conspicuous and slightly swollen. Ventral marginal plate (inferomarginal) projects beyond the aboral marginal plate (superomarginal), defining the edge of the body when viewed from above. Associated colour description: Bright orange to reddish orange. Associated size description: Mostly 70-100 mm; can reach up to 150 mm diameter. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa to East London, from ± 110 m to 630 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Psilaster acuminatus (Pale orange fine-grained star) from the species identification page. PDF page 414, printed page 411. Associated diagnostic features: Leathery star with fine disc plates (paxillae), distinct marginal plates with dividing grooves. Marginal plates become more ‘rolled’ inwards towards the distal (end) part of the arms. Raised node in centre of disc (anal cone). Madreporite is evident. Long arms tapering to narrow, pointed tips. No obvious projecting spines visible to the naked eye. The tube feet are pointed and occur in two rows. Associated colour description: Pale orange to dark pink. Associated size description: Up to 180 mm diameter across arms. Smaller individuals 40-50 mm width. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa, 155-550 m or deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

Associated diagnostic features: Leathery star with fine disc plates (paxillae), distinct marginal plates with dividing grooves. Marginal plates become more ‘rolled’ inwards towards the distal (end) part of the arms. Raised node in centre of disc (anal cone). Madreporite is evident. Long arms tapering to narrow, pointed tips. No obvious projecting spines visible to the naked eye. The tube feet are pointed and occur in two rows. Associated colour description: Pale orange to dark pink. Associated size description: Up to 180 mm diameter across arms. Smaller individuals 40-50 mm width. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa, 155-550 m or deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Plutonaster cf. intermedius (Intermediate starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 415, printed page 412. Associated diagnostic features: Arms moderate length, narrow, tapering more abruptly in the basal part than beyond, tips blunt. Terminal plates more or less truncated (cut short); paxillae (plates) with low rounded columns crowned with 12-30 short spinelets, which emerge directly from the marginal plate. Madreporite covered with paxillae. Stiff, inflexible starfish. Specimens seldom encountered in trawls and are needed for confirming identification. Associated colour description: Pale orange with white marginal plates. Associated size description: Average ± 80 mm diameter, but larger up to 150 mm diameter have been recorded. Associated distribution: Occurs on West and South Coasts of South Africa, around 350 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cheiraster hirsutus (Spiky orange centre star) from the species identification page. PDF page 416, printed page 413. Associated diagnostic features: Tips of arms often curled at ends. Numerous spines, both small and larger, protrude from aboral marginal edge. Long, thin, tapering arms. Double rows of tube feet. Single aboral spine shorter than oral (underside) spines. Two oral (underside) spines, one nearly twice the length of the other. Associated colour description: Ranging from light to dark pink and pale to bright orange. Associated size description: Up to 110 mm diameter. Disc 20 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Predominantly West Coast region of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Stegnobrisinga splendens (Brisingid rigid) from the species identification page. PDF page 417, printed page 414. Associated diagnostic features: Characterised by having a small, circular disc, sharply differentiated from long, slender, rigid and usually deciduous arms (arms fall off), always more than five, usually between 11 to 14. Tube feet suckered in two rows. More rigid, calcified skeleton with raised, ridged markings (furrows) along arms. Associated colour description: Orange, with white ridges. Associated size description: Arms up to 200 mm long, disc up to 30 mm diameter. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Deep-water species 800-4 000 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Henricia abyssalis (Apricot puffy-arm star) from the species identification page. PDF page 418, printed page 415. Associated diagnostic features: Small disc; long, tapering, ‘puffy’ arms. Whitened arm tips that often curl in at ends. Arms and disc inflated (puffy). Small papillae cover entire disc and arms. Aboral surface appears covered in very fine mesh work. Madreporite located midway between centre and arm edge. Two rows of tube feet. Associated colour description: Pale yellow, pale orange, apricot or bright orange. Associated size description: Average 80 mm diameter; up to 175 mm diameter. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa, 56-408 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

Associated diagnostic features: Appears similar to Henricia abyssalis, however surface texture is described as irregular-honeycombed. Arms long and tapering, with small disc. Associated distribution: Occurs predominantly on South Coast, South Africa. Intertidal to 90 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Gilbertaster anacanthus (Gilbert’s star) from the species identification page. PDF page 420, printed page 417. Associated diagnostic features: Arms long and narrow, tapering abruptly at the base and then very slightly throughout to the blunt tip. Disc is of fair size and central part of arms often inflated. Marginal plates are well rounded in shape. No spines of any description occur on general body surface. Very large (1.5 mm), bivalved pedicellaria (claw-shaped structure) present on aboral and oral surfaces, but not on marginal plates. Each marginal plate is covered with close-set, superficially flat, large, irregular granules. Granules around the border of the plate are smaller and form in irregular patterns. Associated colour description: Orange to red. Associated size description: 165 mm diameter and bigger. Associated distribution: One specimen collected from South Coast, South Africa (2014) at 638 m. This species is known primarily from the tropical North Pacific (Hawaiian Islands area). Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Calliaster acanthodes (Spiky sheriff star) from the species identification page. PDF page 421, printed page 418. Associated diagnostic features: Long, sharp and very distinct marginal spines along outer edges, with smaller spines covering the aboral surface. Distinct marginal plates separated by grooves, with long spines emerging from each aboral and oral plate. Pentagon-shaped central disc, but with elongated arms. Six to nine slender furrow spines. Strong, sharp spines on the marginal edges. Associated colour description: Orange, with brown markings on central disc. Associated size description: Up to ± 120 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: South African endemic. South to East Coasts of South Africa. Not usually found on West Coast. Occur at depths between ~ 130 and 420 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Calliaster baccatus (Blunt sheriff star) from the species identification page. PDF page 422, printed page 419. Associated diagnostic features: Pentagon-shaped central disc, with elongated arms ending in bluntly rounded tips. Marginal plates square shaped and conspicuous. Calliaster baccatus has three to four furrow spines on plates lining the tube feet grooves. Blunt, bullet-shaped spines on the marginal edges and aboral surface (but no sharp spines present). Pedicellariae are rare or absent. Associated colour description: Orange, brick red to brown colouration and frequently mottled in colour. Associated size description: Up to ± 100 mm in diameter. Associated distribution: South African endemic. South to East Coasts of South Africa. Not usually found on West Coast. Occur at depths between ~ 10 and 23 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax (Shiny red sheriff star) from the species identification page. PDF page 423, printed page 420. Associated diagnostic features: Well-defined marginal plates separated by grooves. Rigid body with slightly inflated areas over the midradial ridge. Pentagon-shaped with short, webbed arms. Double rows of tube feet. Tips of each arm with a white plate. Often smooth and shiny aboral surface. Associated colour description: Bright red to orange, with pale tips at end of each arm. Pale white to yellow oral surface. Associated size description: Up to 70 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, 150-462 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cladaster macrobrachius (Macro-clad starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 424, printed page 421. Associated diagnostic features: Stellate-shaped with well-developed, pronounced arms tapering to rounded tips. Two rows of tube feet. Marginal plates, square in shape, are covered by widely spaced, coarse granules. In preservation, these granules rub off readily and leave pits. Body is well calcified, i.e. quite rigid. Broad-valved pedicellaria (claw-shape structure) clearly visible on oral surface. Associated colour description: Pale orange, with white areas and white pedicellaria on aboral, becoming paler to white towards edges and tips of arms. Associated size description: ± 60 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. Recorded on West and South Coasts of South Africa, but rarely encountered. Depth recorded from 420 to 914 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Hippasteria phrygiana (Thorny starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 425, printed page 422. Associated diagnostic features: A pentagonal-shaped starfish with fairly short, less pronounced arms. Marginal plates are large, smooth and conspicuous in aboral view and have one or two pronounced, stout spines emerging from each marginal plate. There are no spines on the aboral surface, which has a coarsely granulated appearance. On the oral surface large, obvious clam-shaped pedicellaria are present. Associated colour description: Brick red to orange. Associated size description: Up to 260 mm diameter, but small individuals likely to occur. Associated distribution: Mostly occur on South Coast of South Africa, from 310 to 980 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Hippasteria falklandica (Falkland starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 426, printed page 423. Associated diagnostic features: A pentagonal-shaped starfish with fairly pronounced arms. Marginal plates are large; smooth granules which are conspicuous in aboral view but do not have marginal spines. There are no spines on the aboral surface, which has a coarsely granulated appearance. On the oral and aboral surface large, obvious, clam-shaped pedicellaria are present. Associated colour description: Orange. Associated size description: Up to 130 mm diameter recorded, but small individuals likely to occur. Associated distribution: Mostly occurring on South Coast of South Africa. Known from depths of 149-1 148 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Mediaster bairdi capensis (Orange sheriff star) from the species identification page. PDF page 427, printed page 424. Associated diagnostic features: Commonly occurring inflexible, rigid star with broad disc. Marginal plates distinct, block-shaped and covered with granules, separated by grooves on upper surface. Tube feet end in a blunt sucker tip. Disc plates distinct and large, with distinct checkerboard appearance. Arms taper narrowly and immediately. Associated colour description: Orange to red. content: Distribution map for Toraster tuberculatus (Red sheriff star Inflated state) from the species identification page. PDF page 428, printed page 425. Associated diagnostic features: Commonly occurring, rigid starfish with broad disc and short arms. Pentagonal to stellate in body shape. Distinct bald tubercles cover the entire aboral surface. Marginal plates distinct, granulated and separated by grooves on upper surface. Distinct madreporite. Distal plates (towards arm tips) often swollen or enlarged. Arm tips vary from either sharply pointed to bluntly rounded. Abactinal plates larger in size along radial lines. Ventral plates covered with granules. Body of starfish sometimes inflated when landed from a trawl net, but deflates over time. Associated colour description: Red, brown, dark orange on aboral; pale cream to yellow on oral side. Associated size description: Up to 160 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa. Has been reported from Durban area. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Luidia sarsii africana (Legs break easily starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 429, printed page 426. Associated diagnostic features: Arms usually break off central disc very easily. Distinct spines protrude from aboral margin edge; arms long, flexible, flattened and tapering, strap-like. Usually five arms. Associated colour description: Brown to dark pink. Associated size description: Average up to 150 mm diameter, but can get larger individuals. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, to Port Elizabeth; 54 m to 360+ m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Chondraster elattosis (Pentagon star) from the species identification page. PDF page 430, printed page 427. Associated diagnostic features: Inflexible, rigid star with thick, solid, spongy disc. Pentagonal in shape. Marginal plates indistinct. Distinct madreporite. Fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) form distinct rows along each arm, but no spines apparent. Thick fleshy starfish with smooth aboral and oral surface. Double rows of tube feet. No marginal plates visible. Patterning on aboral surface can be very distinct when brooding (see third image). Associated colour description: Bright pink to orange on aboral; pale yellow on oral surface. Associated size description: Can reach up to 230 mm diameter. Associated distribution: South African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 400 to 1 000+ m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Spoladaster veneris (Inflated star) from the species identification page. PDF page 431, printed page 428. content: Specimen detail reference image for Poraniopsis echinaster (Spiky cushion star) from the species identification page. PDF page 432, printed page 429. Associated diagnostic features: Short-armed, stellate body form with a reticular skeleton (spiky skeleton with soft tissue covering). Distinct raised spines covering the aboral surface 1-4 mm in length. Arms fairly rigid, with ends often turning upwards or curling inwards. Two rows of tube feet. Madreporite white in colour, located off- centre halfway to base of arms. Strong spines along the base of arms. Associated colour description: Deep orange to red or even pure white, with spines light red to yellowish white. Pale oral surface. Associated size description: Average 50 up to 160 mm diameter, mostly small specimens but occasionally large too. Associated distribution: South Atlantic including West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pseudarchaster tessellatus (Dusky pink long-armed star) from the species identification page. PDF page 433, printed page 430. Associated diagnostic features: Inflexible star with broad disc and long, tapering, rigid arms. Disc plates distinct, regular oval/circular in shape. Fine texture on aboral plates, but plates begin to separate once out of water. Distinct marginal plates on both aboral and oral sides. Two rows of tube feet mostly hidden by fine clusters of spines on the inside oral margin of each arm. Madreporite midway between disc centre and marginal plate. Associated colour description: Dusky pink to white. Associated size description: Average 70 mm diameter, but up to 160 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Diplopteraster multipes (Large prickly slime cushion star) from the species identification page. PDF page 435, printed page 432. Associated diagnostic features: Large, fleshy and inflated disc with cover of skin supported by spines. Tips of arms appear upturned and white. Flesh ‘decomposes’ rapidly when on deck, resulting in mushy texture and production of a lot of mucus. Best to keep specimens in dish of water until ready to discard. Four rows of tube feet visible in wide tube foot grooves. Associated colour description: Pale orange, bright orange to red. Associated size description: Up to 200-260 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Throughout West and South Coast region of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Diplopteraster multipes (Large prickly slime cushion star) from the species identification page. PDF page 435, printed page 432. content: Specimen detail reference image for Pteraster capensis (Common/Brooding cushion star) from the species identification page. PDF page 436, printed page 433. Associated diagnostic features: Small, puffy cushion starfish with fairly solid texture. Produce a lot of mucus when disturbed (also called Slime Stars). Plates appear as fine rosettes of holes covering aboral surface. Ends of arms turned upwards and have white tips. Specimens range in size from very tiny (20 mm diameter) to very large (150 mm diameter). Associated colour description: Wide range of colours – pink, yellow, orange, brown, mottled. In deeper waters usually white, but colour variation of orange occurs on South Coast. Associated size description: Average 20-25 mm; can be larger up to 135+ mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Lophaster quadrispinus (Four-spined starfish) from the species identification page. PDF page 437, printed page 434. Associated diagnostic features: Many raised tubercles (paxillae) covering entire aboral surface in symmetric pattern. Fairly rigid star and arms usually bent stiffly when on deck. Marginal edge with extended paxillae distinct and small tufts on tips. Associated colour description: Pale to bright orange to red. Associated size description: Average 50 mm diameter, but larger specimens can occur. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Crossaster penicillatus (Raspberry star/Blomme) from the species identification page. PDF page 438, printed page 435. Associated diagnostic features: Wide flattened disc with 9 to 12 arms. Bundles of spines on aboral surface. Soft-bodied starfish with flexible spines. Associated size description: Average 70 mm diameter; up to 120 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Throughout West and South Coast region of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Odontaster australis (False sheriff star) from the species identification page. PDF page 440, printed page 437. Associated diagnostic features: Fairly rigid star with distinct marginal plates and slightly inflated disc and arms. Madreporite located off-centre, as a clearly distinguishable light spot. Wider marginal plates distinct; oral surface plates have spinules (rather than granules, as in Mediaster); fewer spines and distinct plates surrounding mouth opening. Odontaster spp. have 5 x single, long, sharply tapered teeth visible on oral surface surrounding the mouth opening (see photo) = distinguishing feature between Odontaster spp. and Mediaster spp. Associated colour description: Ranging from pale yellow to orange to red. Associated size description: Average 70-80 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. Known from 320 m Saldanha Bay, West Coast of South Africa. Rarely encountered in trawl surveys. Extracted image dimensions: 192x148.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Comanthus wahlbergii (Common feather star/Crinoid) from the species identification page. PDF page 441, printed page 438. Associated diagnostic features: Between 10 and 22 segmented arms that originate from a small, central disc, below which are cirri which attach the animal to the seafloor or rock. Arms have a feather-like appearance with side branches or pinnules. Associated colour description: White, pink, orange to pale brown or yellow, often variegated. Associated size description: Arms can be up to 150 mm in length. Associated distribution: South-western Cape, South Coast and southern reach of East Coast of South Africa. Shallow to ± 60 m and possibly deeper. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Goniocidaris (Goniocidaris) indica (Umbrella urchin) from the species identification page. PDF page 442, printed page 439. content: Specimen detail reference image for Stereocidaris excavata (Pencil urchin) from the species identification page. PDF page 443, printed page 440. Associated diagnostic features: Large, robust urchin. Sturdy, long, slender, serrated, flute-like primary spines (although often easily detach from test). Darkened secondary spines encircling base of primary spines. Dark, double rows of miliary spines, extending from top to bottom of test (ambulacrum). Anal area, sunken with centrally positioned, elevated pores. Associated colour description: Beige to brown, with darkened secondary spines at base of primary spine and darkened ambulacrum. May have a green tint. Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 69 mm. Associated distribution: Endemic to the South Coast of South Africa; 120-170 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Histocidaris purpurata (Purple pencil urchin) from the species identification page. PDF page 444, printed page 441. Associated diagnostic features: Round, robust test. Long, robust, pointy primary spines with darkened smooth base and lighter ridged extensions. Secondary spines considerably shorter, flattened, narrowing to a blunt tip. Associated colour description: Brown underlying test and brown to red secondary spines. Base of primary spines deep purplish-red, with contrasting pale pink to white at tips. Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 28 mm. Associated distribution: South Coast of South Africa, and globally North Atlantic, Indian Ocean and New Zealand; 750-1080 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Hygrosoma petersii (Grey Tam O'Shanter) from the species identification page. PDF page 445, printed page 442. content: Specimen detail reference image for Phormosoma placenta africana (Beret urchin/Tam O'Shanter) from the species identification page. PDF page 446, printed page 443. Associated diagnostic features: Soft, flexible, disc-shaped test, texture leather- like, usually collapsed in trawl. Deepened areoles (circular areas around spine-bearing structure). Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 120 mm. Associated distribution: Endemic to the West Coast of South Africa; at 50-3 700 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Dermechinus horridus africanus (Orange pumpkin urchin) from the species identification page. PDF page 447, printed page 444. Associated diagnostic features: Globular, delicate and extremely high test (pumpkin- like appearance), becoming more vertically raised with age. Slender, fragile, sparsely arranged spines that readily detach from test. Primary spines longer than secondary ones. Distinct white tubercles in rows from oral to aboral sides. Associated colour description: Bright, sometimes pale, orange to red. Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 90 mm; maximum height 120 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coast region of South Africa, Pacific and Antarctica; 30-1 020 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Echinus gilchristi (Spiky/common sea urchin) from the species identification page. PDF page 448, printed page 445. Associated diagnostic features: Round test, dorsally compressed and wider laterally (short, squat). Thin, hollow, brittle spines readily broken in trawl net. Mouth with protruding teeth and fleshy lip around opening. Associated colour description: Test brownish to pink and sometimes greenish, primary spines uniform white, green or pale pink, secondary spines red-brownish, sometimes greenish. Distinct darker bands in double rows running from dorsal to ventral side. Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 84 mm. Associated distribution: Endemic to the West and South Coast region of South Africa; at 40-500 m depth range. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Brissopsis lyrifera capensis (Brissopsis/heart urchin) from the species identification page. PDF page 451, printed page 448. Associated diagnostic features: Elongated, heart-shaped test, with distinct frontal notch. Petals straight, divergent, anterior ones longer than posterior. Thin, short, fragile uniform spines, generally fall off in trawl net. Some specimens with distinct darker brown/black fasciole in shape of lyre on dorsal surface, but not all individuals have this marking. Associated colour description: Brown, with some individuals (but not all) having a distinct darker line in shape of lyre. Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 70 mm. Associated distribution: Endemic to the West and South Coast region of South Africa; 5-1 400 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Echinocardium cordatum (Small heart urchin/ Sea potato) from the species identification page. PDF page 452, printed page 449. Associated diagnostic features: Moderately high, oval-shaped test, with deepened anterior notch, frequently with a red colouration. Distinctive, wide petals, with conspicuous pores containing tube feet. Anterior petals longer than posterior ones. Spines closely packed, directed backwards. Associated colour description: White to pale beige/cream, sometimes with red colouration around the frontal notch. Associated size description: Maximum horizontal diameter 90 mm. Associated distribution: Cosmopolitan species, reported along the entire coast of South Africa; from littoral to 230 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Ophiocreas spp. (Brown-skinned snake star) from the species identification page. PDF page 454, printed page 451. Associated diagnostic features: Moderate in size, often attached onto other marine life when landed on deck. Arms do not branch but curl considerably, thick at bases and most of arms, thin at arm tips. Whole animal covered in thin skin, which easily tears off when damaged. Associated colour description: Light brown, becoming darker towards arm tips. White beneath skin. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 30 mm. Arms very long, but tightly curled. Associated distribution: Unknown. Only two specimens encountered to date. Further specimens and taxonomy required. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Astrothorax waitei (Apricot basket star) from the species identification page. PDF page 455, printed page 452. Associated diagnostic features: Small size, often attached onto other marine life (sea fans or sponges) when landed on deck. Disc swollen (tumid), dorsal surface and arms banded. Both dorsal and ventral sides covered in coarse and fine tubercles intermixed, ventral tubercles abruptly finer. Jaws also covered by fine tubercles. Arms five, long, do not branch, but may be tightly coiled dorso-ventrally. Arm spines, up to ten, with shape changing from thorny-tipped stumps proximally to F-shaped hooks distally. Associated colour description: Pale orange, apricot. Associated size description: Considerably smaller than other basket stars, disc diameter up to 20 mm. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa to East Coast, Durban; 0-1 005 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Astrocladus euryale (Black and white basket star) from the species identification page. PDF page 456, printed page 453. Associated diagnostic features: Disc round, smooth. Radial shields armed with moderate to large round tubercles, which continue down arms but are absent at arm tips. Arms branch at disc margin. Arms readily detach and a tangled mass of arms may be the only parts retained. Ventral disc smooth and naked, including jaws and oral area. Oral papillae spiniform, fringe oral area including distal notches. Arm spines on ventral side of arms, conical, becoming hook-shaped towards arm tips. Associated colour description: Mainly black and white and/or grey with black surrounding tubercles on disc and arms, disc colour sometimes olive green. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 75 mm. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast, off Cape Town to East Coast, central KwaZulu-Natal; 11-555 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 146x146.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Astrodendrum capensis (Purple basket star) from the species identification page. PDF page 457, printed page 454. Associated diagnostic features: Disc round, few scattered tubercles in between radial shields. Radial shields armed with small to moderate tubercles, which are fat at their bases but pointed at their tips. Tubercles do not continue down arms. Arms branch extensively from disc margin. Ventral disc smooth and naked, sometimes with small tubercles. Oral papillae spiniform, fringe oral area excluding in distal notches. Associated colour description: Purple or reddish, may have a few white speckles on main area of disc. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 95 mm. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Kosi Bay, South Africa. Depth range 161-420 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Gorgonocephalus chilensis (red/chilean basket star) from the species identification page. PDF page 458, printed page 455. Associated diagnostic features: Disc slightly inflated; dorsal areas between radial shields slightly indented. Radial shields conspicuous, narrow, densely covered in conical tubercles; remainder of disc covered in skin with numerous scattered tubercles, sometimes smaller in size. Disc margin with few larger tubercles. Ventral interradial areas covered in skin with small, scattered, low tubercles, few scattered tubercles towards oral area. Five arms, branching from or within disc. Arms readily detach and tangled mass of arms may be the only parts retained. Oral papillae and teeth spiniform, fringe oral frame, but absent in distal notches. Associated colour description: Brick red, pink to light brown in colour, with white speckles. Associated size description: Up to 64 mm disc diameter. Associated distribution: West Coast, off Cape Town to East Coast, Port Edward; 22-900 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Gorgonocephalus pustulatum (Brown basket star) from the species identification page. PDF page 459, printed page 456. Associated diagnostic features: Dorsal disc covering variable, sometimes naked interradially, while others with many tubercles, conical or almost spine-like. Radial shields narrow, with irregular tubercles. Ventral surface flat, covered in tubercles or may be naked. Oral papillae and teeth slender, spiniform, forming continuous fringe, but not within distal notches. Arms, five, branching from or within disc. Arms readily detach and tangled mass of arms may be the only parts retained. Associated colour description: Brown to pink-brown with white speckles. Centre of disc dark. Associated size description: Up to 54 mm disc diameter. Associated distribution: West Coast of South Africa to beyond East London; 78-860 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Cryptopelta aster (red and white banded brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 460, printed page 457. Associated diagnostic features: Distinct red-and-white-banded arms with a red floret-patterned (flower-patterned) central disc. Disc pentagonal, flat, covered both dorsally and ventrally in fine granules extending onto first few arm segments. Arm spines up to seven, sometimes eight, less than half segment length. Associated colour description: Floret pattern red to orange and white, arms banded. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 13 mm. Arms relatively short, three times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, reaching to East Coast, north of Durban; 75-421 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Cryptopelta aster (red and white banded brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 460, printed page 457. Associated diagnostic features: Distinct red-and-white-banded arms with a red floret-patterned (flower-patterned) central disc. Disc pentagonal, flat, covered both dorsally and ventrally in fine granules extending onto first few arm segments. Arm spines up to seven, sometimes eight, less than half segment length. Associated colour description: Floret pattern red to orange and white, arms banded. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 13 mm. Arms relatively short, three times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, reaching to East Coast, north of Durban; 75-421 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Ophiothrix aristulata (Feathery brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 461, printed page 458. Associated diagnostic features: Disc round or pentagonal, disc scales on the central disc are more or less obscured by spines, spinelets or thorny stumps. Radial shields triangular, large and naked. Arms are mainly horizontally flexible (side-to-side movement) and have minimal dorso- ventral (up and down) movement. Distinct white stripe down arms. Arm spines, up to ten, usually long (six times arm segment length), glassy, more or less serrated and tapering, lower spines short and often just stumps. Species very active on deck, readily flipping from dorsal to ventral sides. Frequently associated with sponges. Associated colour description: Disc usually darker than arms, colours vary from orange, grey, red to pink. Arms with light white longitudinal line, sometimes with pink or red stripes bordering the line. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 16 mm. Arms long, nine times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Sodwana Bay; usually more than 200 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Ophiothrix fragilis (Bristly brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 462, printed page 459. Associated diagnostic features: Dorsal disc covered in thorny spinelets, stumps and spines; may be intermixed. Radial shields large, covered with spines. Arm spines up to ten, glassy, thorny over total length, not tapering, sometimes lowermost spine transformed into a hook, longest spine not more than three times segment length. Long spines protrude along the margins of the length of the arms, giving a ‘feathery’ appearance. Tips of the arms are readily discarded when disturbed. Shallow, abundant species. Associated colour description: Orange to red, often with darker brown, grey or purple central disc. May have various combinations of oranges, reds, greens, greys, browns, purples, yellows and pinks. Arms banded and often with dots associated with dorsal arm plates longitudinally along arms. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 20 mm. Arms moderate in length, three to five times disc diameter. Associated distribution: West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Kosi Bay; less than 100 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ophiolycus dentatus (Toothed brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 463, printed page 460. Associated diagnostic features: Disc pentagonal, covered in thick skin. Radial shields narrow, just shorter than width of arm base, not distinct. Oral papillae spiniform, long. Teeth similar in shape, but smaller and clustered at apex of jaw. Arms five, simple, length moderate. Dorsal arm plates fragmented especially basally, covered by thick skin. Arm spines three, lowermost cigar- shaped, broad and flattened, approximately one segment length, remaining spines spiniform, uppermost being slightly longer than segment length, distal spines becoming hook-shaped. Often damaged in sample. Associated colour description: Red to orange dorsally, lighter ventrally. Colouration sometimes fades to white from trawl damage. Arms red, mottled. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms three times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West Coast (Groen river) to East Coast (Sodwana Bay) of South Africa; 129-450 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis (Bright red disc brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 464, printed page 461. Associated diagnostic features: Bright red/orange in colour. Disc pentagonal, covered with thick, smooth, glossy skin. Radial shields short, but not distinct in fresh specimens. Oral papillae three to four, broad, serrated, flattened, with transparent edges. Teeth similar, four to five. Arms five, moderately long, flexible and tapered, mottled in colouration, also covered in thick skin. Arm spines slender, serrated and rugose at tip, up to four on free segments. Disintegrates quickly out of water and is often severely damaged in trawls. Associated colour description: Bright glossy red, yellow or orange disc, mottled red/ orange/white arms. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms three to four times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast off Orange River to East Coast, East London; 101-450 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms three to four times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast off Orange River to East Coast, East London; 101-450 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 146x146.

content: Distribution map for Ophiomisidium pulchellum (Spiky orange brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 466, printed page 463. Associated diagnostic features: Very small species, seldom encountered. Disc round, disc scales large, thick and taking up most of dorsal disc. Radial shields oval. Oral papillae two, fused each side of triangular apical papillae. Arms rigid, short, consisting of approximately 15 segments only. Spiky in appearance due to spines on arms and disc. Arm spines three, enlarged, flattened, blunt, and rapidly decreasing in size down arm. Associated colour description: Pale orange. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 5 mm. Arms one to two times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: West Coast, off Cape Town to East Coast, south of Durban; 70-3 065 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ophiura (Ophiura) trimeni (Orange stripe brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 467, printed page 464. Associated diagnostic features: Disc scales covered in thin skin. Radial shields half disc radius, twice as long as wide, not touching. Mouth or oral slit usually wide open, oral papillae three, distalmost broadest, apical papillae pointed. Teeth three to five, same shape as apical papillae. Arm spines three, spines twice segment length, one segment length towards end of arms. Orange and white longitudinal striped arms. Patterned disc with orange and white shapes. Very small, fragile species. Very common and abundant. Associated colour description: Orange and white. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 9 mm. Arms three to four times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Sodwana Bay; 165-1 647 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Distribution map for Ophiura (Ophiuroglypha) costata costata (rigid orange brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 468, printed page 465. Associated diagnostic features: Arms and disc inflexible (rigid), arms often broken. Disc pentagonal, disc scales distinct, thick, irregular, forming star shape on disc edged in darker orange colour. Radial shields longer than wide, oval, separated by scales. Mouth narrow or tightly closed. Arms fairly long when unbroken, can be more than four times disc diameter. Arm spines three, very short and appressed to arm. Associated colour description: Orange to orange-red. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms often broken, but can be more than four times disc diameter. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast, off Orange River to South Coast, Cape St Francis; 43-1 647 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Ophiactis abyssicola (Abyss brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 469, printed page 466. Associated diagnostic features: Disc round, sparsely scattered conical spines on disc, concentrated on margin. Radial shields naked, oblong to rectangular. Arms five, simple, long, moniliform (like string of beads) distally. Three to four arm spines, erect, may be pointed or blunt, cylindrical, middle spine longest, half to two times longer than segment. Associated colour description: Orange arms with darker purple, grey or brown disc; some specimens with a pinkish tinge. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 8 mm. Arms three to eight times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: West Coast, off Cape Columbine to South Coast off Still Bay; 167-2 743 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Ophiactis carnea (Fleshy brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 470, printed page 467. Associated diagnostic features: Arms five, simple. Disc round, covered in spines, sometimes with darkened area or blotch in centre of disc visible. Radial shields naked, elongated D-shaped, moderate in size. Three to five arm spines. Associated colour description: Reddish brown to pink, brown or orange, sometimes with white patches. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 6 mm. content: Distribution map for Ophiomitrella hamata (coal stack brittle star) from the species identification page. PDF page 471, printed page 468. Associated diagnostic features: Very small species, disc round and covered with short blunt stumps. Radial shields oval in shape, short. Associated size description: Disc diameter up to 4 mm. Arms three times disc diameter in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. South Coast, off Mossel Bay to East Coast, Durban; 63-900 m depth. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Thyone venusta (Orange and white speckled sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 472, printed page 469. Associated diagnostic features: U-shaped body, cylindrical, with posterior end turned upward. Skin smooth, but appears ‘hairy’ due to numerous scattered fine tube feet (podia). Speckled orange and white colour, darker dorsally. Associated colour description: White, speckled with orange. Associated size description: 90-100 mm in length, width 8-10 mm. Associated distribution: South Coast of South Africa, extending to southern East Coast. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Hemiocnus insolens (red-chested sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 473, printed page 470. Associated diagnostic features: Small, solid sea cucumber distinguished by its bright colours red or yellow, although white variations are also common, especially on the West Coast. Solid, slightly gelatinous texture. Tube feet scattered all round. Ten irregularly branched tentacles. Usually occurs in dense colonies, especially on the West Coast. Associated colour description: Usually bright red, yellow or white, but can vary. Associated size description: 25-60 mm in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa as far east as Port Elizabeth. Intertidal to 110 m. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.
