content: Specimen detail reference image for Psolus griffithsi (Scaled sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 474, printed page 471. Associated diagnostic features: Distinct species identifiable by the dorsal scales covering the body and the sucker-like ventral surface forming a sole. Scales overlapping and covered with minute granules. Tentacles are bushy when visible. Tube feet (podia) present on ventral sole in two rows; outer row minute and inner row much larger. Associated colour description: Beige scales with orange/brown centres, ventral sole grey to brown. Associated size description: 20-25 mm length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pseudostichopus langeae (Sand covered sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 475, printed page 472. Associated diagnostic features: Cylindrical body form with ventral surface slightly flattened and dorsal surface slightly arched. Thick, leathery and smooth body wall, usually encrusted with sand grains, broken shells, coral debris, echinoid spines and foraminifera, but no pteropod shells or sponge spicules. Tiny tube feet (podia) mostly along dorso-lateral edges. Retains firm shape out of water. Mouth located on ventral surface with between 18 and 20 peltate (leaf- or shield-shaped) projecting tentacles, cream to brown in colour. Anus located sub-ventrally in a distinct pygal (posterior) furrow. Associated colour description: Skin is covered in sand grains, but when the encrustations are washed off, the skin is opaque, off-white to cream in colour. Associated size description: Up to 70 mm in length, 8-10 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, ranging in depth from ± 100-400 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Zygothuria lactea (Slimy deep-water sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 476, printed page 473. Associated diagnostic features: Very slimy, soft body wall with folded outer skin that readily disintegrates off main body. Has 20 pink to orange-coloured tentacles visible at mouth. Tube feet greatly reduced and difficult to detect. Associated colour description: Light brown to mud-coloured outer skin layer, with pale pink to white body wall. Associated size description: Up to 140 mm in length. Associated distribution: Deeper waters – three individual specimens captured at 369, 617 and 907 m on West coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Synallactes mollis (South coast purple sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 477, printed page 474. Associated diagnostic features: Gelatinous, slimy body wall with thin outer brown skin layer (frequently torn) covering pale purple body wall beneath. Maintains shape on trawl deck but not rigid. Tube feet variable in size, decreasing in size posteriorly. A double ring of 16 to 22 tentacles present. Associated colour description: Brown outer skin to purple body wall with darker tube feet. content: Specimen detail reference image for Synallactes viridilimus (Purple sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 478, printed page 475. Associated diagnostic features: Large gelatinous body, often slimy. Thin body wall. Mouth with 20 peltate (leaf- or shield-shaped) crown of tentacles, orange to yellow in colour. Upper tentacles in single row, lower tentacles in double row. Ventro-lateral tube feet (podia) more prominent and longer than mid-ventral tube feet. Associated colour description: Brown to pale purple in colour. Tube feet darker purple. Associated size description: Up to 450 mm in length. Associated distribution: Endemic. West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Synallactes sp. (Large lilac sea cucumber) from the species identification page. PDF page 479, printed page 476. Associated diagnostic features: Large gelatinous body wall coated in substantial slime that is readily rubbed off along with body wall tissue. Retains shape out of water, but body wall tissue not very robust to handling and is easily damaged. Only one specimen recorded to date. Associated colour description: Pale purple/lilac colour with darker oral and anal areas. Associated size description: Approximately 300 mm in length. Associated distribution: Only one specimen recorded from trawl 710 m depth on West Coast of South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Ascidia incrassata (Orange sea squirt) from the species identification page. PDF page 484, printed page 481. Associated diagnostic features: Characteristic red bands between each of the eight branchial siphon lobes. Colouration of lobes may be obscured when the siphons are closed and retracted. Both siphons, situated on the anterior part of the elongated globular body, may be covered in varying degrees of mud and sand. Body colour varies from red to orange and yellow. If no bands occur, photograph and retain specimen. Associated colour description: Orange to red or even purple tunic (outer body wall), with red bands on inside of siphon. Associated size description: Up to 140 mm. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa to Mozambique. Recorded as invasive off the Pacific Coast of Panama. Intertidally to a depth of 114 m. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pyura stolonifera (red bait) from the species identification page. PDF page 485, printed page 482. Associated diagnostic features: Large, solitary ascidian, commonly found in aggregations or groups. Leathery, slightly wrinkled tunic, beige to brown to dark slate coloured, bare or with various attached epibionts. Siphons large, anteriorly placed, with distinct, slightly scalloped edges forming four lobes. Pointy papillae NOT present at the base of the siphons. Associated colour description: Beige to slate black, with sides or areas more brown orange in colour. May be heavily overgrown with algae, sponges and other ascidians. Interior test whitish, with orange to red viscera. Associated size description: Large, typically 150 mm in height, but can grow considerably larger than this. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, very widespread off southern Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Gynandrocarpa placenta (Elephants ears ascidian) from the species identification page. PDF page 486, printed page 483. Associated diagnostic features: Short wrinkled stalk or peduncle supporting an oval to large, laterally flatted disc-shaped head. Test of the head is cartilaginous and firm, tunic white, cream or pink in colour. Siphon apertures of embedded zooids distinctly visible on colony surface. Stalk often encrusted with epibionts, especially hydroids. Associated colour description: White to pink tunic; zooids pink in life; pale orange brown peduncle. Associated size description: Colonies can reach up to 200 mm in length, but mostly small individuals retained in trawls. May also be found on the carapace of crabs, e.g. Pseudodromia latens. Associated distribution: South Coast, South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 192x192.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pseudodistoma spp. (Soft lightbulb ascidian) from the species identification page. PDF page 487, printed page 484. Associated diagnostic features: Soft gelatinous body with distinct stalk, lightly impregnated with fine sand particles visible through the milky transparent test. Zooids visible through the test of globular head, patterned arrangement may or may not be visible. Stalk attached by root-like structures to the substrate. Associated colour description: Translucent/opaque white and light brown (due to the presence of sand particles within test of stalk). White zooids are visible through milky test, brown dots are faecal matter contained within the zooid gut. Associated size description: Variable, from 20-100 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Wide distribution. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Aplidium spp. (Sandy club ascidian) from the species identification page. PDF page 488, printed page 485. Associated diagnostic features: Soft, gelatinous head with or without small amount of fine sand particles. Long, thin but firmer sandy stalk; may be slightly enlarged; attaches to substrate. Associated size description: Stalk 50 mm, head 40 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Wide distribution. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Distaplia spp. (Stalked ascidian) from the species identification page. PDF page 489, printed page 486. Associated diagnostic features: Soft gelatinous body with distinct stalk and attachment “roots” forming a club-type shape. Firm opaque stalk, no sand externally or internally. Attachment may carry several stalked heads. Broader, softer, slightly elongated head. Associated colour description: Whitish zooids, arranged into systems that may or may not be visible. Orange colouration may be visible through semi-transparent test of the head at times when developing ova and larvae are present during the breeding season. Associated size description: Variable, from 20 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Wide distribution. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Whole-specimen reference image for Synoicum spp. (Baseball bat ascidian) from the species identification page. PDF page 490, printed page 487. Associated diagnostic features: Gelatinous sandy body, with distinct stalk and attachment “roots” forming a baseball club-type shape. More rigid and firm structure than other club-shaped ascidians. Sandy texture, with grains of sand coating the outer body wall. Associated colour description: Translucent/opaque brown to pink, often covered with fine sediment. Associated size description: Variable, up to 70 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts, South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Molgula scutata (Sand colonial ascidian) from the species identification page. PDF page 491, printed page 488. Associated diagnostic features: Sand-covered globules of gelatinous mass together forming clusters. Attach to each other and to many other structures, even to polychaete tubes anchored in the sediment. Often attach to the carapace of Exodromidia sp. Associated colour description: When washed and free of sand, the body is opaque/ transparent. Associated size description: Individuals about 20 mm diameter, but together form larger clusters up to 150 mm diameter. Associated distribution: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts, South Africa. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Pyrosoma spp. (Fire roller) from the species identification page. PDF page 492, printed page 489. Associated diagnostic features: Planktonic colonial tunicates, cylindrical, globular or conical shaped. Made up of thousands of zooids embedded in gelatinous tunic. Distinct bumps (zooids) form on the outside of the colony, but the inside is much smoother. Associated colour description: Mottled brown-orange or paler pink, with translucent/opaque body. Associated size description: Variable, ranging from 50 mm to 300 mm. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa throughout water column, very widespread. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Specimen detail reference image for Salpa spp. (Transluscent salps) from the species identification page. PDF page 493, printed page 490. Associated diagnostic features: Pelagic tunicates, often cylindrical. Can be colonial or solitary, but colonies usually break apart in trawl net. Very thin body wall, transparent and slimy, with brown globular intestinal tract and stomach visible. Associated colour description: Transparent or translucent, but with digestive organs or other parts of the musculature visible within the transparent body. Associated size description: Individuals usually up to 60 mm in length. Associated distribution: West and South Coasts of South Africa throughout water column, very widespread. Extracted image dimensions: 191x191.

content: Distribution map for Cephalodiscus gilchristi (Common name Agar animal) from the species identification page. PDF page 496, printed page 493. Associated diagnostic features: Very little is known about this unusual animal. Colonial species harbouring polypides (zooids) within the branched tubes make up the structure of the animal. Tubes joined together at base are thought to provide attachment to substratum. Base larger in diameter than tubes and without spines. Zooids reside in cavities of the branched tubes (tubarium). Juveniles are believed to move through the structures to form new branches. Solid spines occur on the tubarium along with ostia (apertures). Cephalodiscus means ‘disk-head’. Associated colour description: Red-orange to brown. Associated size description: Largest recorded 190 mm in length and 110 mm wide. Associated distribution: South African endemic. Mostly South Coast of South Africa but specimens have been recorded from West Coast. Extracted image dimensions: 156x141.
